`Query.encode()` now supports `quote_plus` in trunk.

```python
>>> f = furl('http://slrp.su/?cool%20path=for%20dads')
>>> f.query.encode(quote_plus=False)
'cool%20path=for%20dads'
```

Does that suffice for your needs?

(v0.4.8) after I add a similar `encode()` or `stringify()` method to `furl`
objects.
Just out of interest: Which part of this change breaks 2.6 compatibility?

Looking at the place where the key error is occurring, I see this method:

```
    def _on_join(self, c, e):
        ch = e.target
        nick = e.source.nick
        if nick == c.get_nickname():
            self.channels[ch] = Channel()
        self.channels[ch].add_user(nick)
```

So it looks like the code is using `nick == self.get_nickname()` to determine if this is self joining the channel (in which case it adds the channel to the dict of channel nicks) or someone else. However, because you've set your nick to '', the join message for yourself looks like someone else joining... and you've never joined.

What should happen here? Should the bot handle '' as a special nick? Should it somehow set the nickname based on the events from the server? Should it store users in a channel without regard for which nick is self? I don't think it can do that and accurately keep track of which channels the bot is in.

I note that Main.py is the scripts/testbot.py file (with modifications).

It reads all lines for me. Perhaps you're on the wrong version? The TransactionDetails class in 'tags.py' should look like this:

```python
class TransactionDetails(Tag):
    '''Transaction details

    Pattern: 6x65x
    '''
    id = 86
    scope = mt940.models.Transaction
    pattern = r'(?P<transaction_details>[\s\S]{0,330})'
```

The format after the `:61:` should look like `6!n[4!n]2a[1!a]15d1!a3!c16x[//16x]` according to the standard: http://www.sepaforcorporates.com/swift-for-corporates/account-statement-mt940-file-format-overview/

In your case the 141231 indicates year 14, month 12 and date 31. The 0105 would be the entry date in months. If it's somehow incorrect than either the standard that I used is incorrect or the ABN AMRO doesn't follow the standard. Both are possible since all banks seem to have their own interpretation

I think I might have come across a similar problem with flower not terminating a process.  The cause was that I was running on Windows 7 using --pool=solo, because celery wasn't sending results back properly when running with the default prefork pool. To fix this I found I had to specify the backend using update config rather than the constructor:

    app.conf.update(
        CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND='redis://localhost:6379/0'
    )

Rather than:

     Celery('tasks', broker='redis://localhost//', backend='redis://localhost:6379/0')

As described here: https://github.com/celery/celery/issues/897.

A followup comment.   I'll take a look at this linking stuff.  There could be some interesting things there.

My main concern:  I don't think that a task should report anything about its status (or termination) to another task except via an explicit `task.join()` operation.    The idea of having tasks linked and cancelled when a another task dies is slightly different issue.  Tasks can already be cancelled at any time in Curio.   So, one could certainly envision different ways of initiating that cancellation.

On 12/20/16, giampaolo <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> On the other hand I see that curio explicitly promotes the usage of a thread
> pool when it comes to dealing with files (`aopen()` etc.).

I'm unaware that Curio promotes any threaded stuff for files, yet happily
using aopen and friends in pure linear curio code. Can you provide some
reference for such promotion?

> - if sendfile() fails on the first call (because fd is not a regular fd),
> internally we fallback on using the plain `chunk = file.read(maxsize) ->
> sock.sendall(chunk)` methodology.

This is for/while loop with pure curio code. So no need to think about side
stepping to a thread.

> - add a `Socket.sendfile(fd, maxsize=65536)` method which by default tries
> to send the file using sendfile()
> - by default `Socket.sendfile` *does not* use a thread pool, but if the user
> is worried about blocking he/she can optionally do so with `await
> run_in_thread(s.sendfile, fd)`.

Native sendfile will block curio until it returns. fd -> Socket
dumping will take
time. So side stepping to a thread is needed.

If "helper task(s)" is the only way, then the question on the
stackoverflow is answered: You need asyncs for this.
Or a helper thread may help. But this may be reason
mainstreams stayed away from it.

This may be interesting find of the day: It means
piping is intrinsically in the land of Curio..

With that, I'm aiming to get all outstanding PRs bundled into a release, and all issues replied to by the end of February.

Most of all, thanks for your contribution to Will, your patience with an imperfect, quite human project maintainer, and for continuing to come back, engage, and spend some of the precious seconds you get on earth making this project better.

I appreciate you.  Thank you.

From my earlier comments, Pandas and Scipy/Numpy's CSV parsers will not be suitable here due to their more restricted requirements.

Other lightweight parsers might be ok, but for now backport.csv seems does the job of unifying the CSV parser behavior between py2 and py3, which is one of the primary concerns here.

Regarding performance, I think memory concerns are more important that speed (If the CSV is in the markdown source, it won't be too big. If it is external file, it will likely be IO bound. But no bound on CSV file size means there's no bound in memory consumption, and Python probably would make this worse.). And I have another method to avoid this issue when filtering is needed.
Note that you'll have to add the 7777 back to each string, since the split will remove it.

Thanks for your contribution! I realize this is your first one, so let's walk through it carefully.

One design decision of rawpy was to provide the minimum possible interface to open RAW images, get access to the unprocessed image (the Bayer matrix data), and provide basic functions to postprocess RAW images (although anyone can just take the RAW data and write custom postprocessing algorithms directly in Python). And all that by abstracting as much as possible over the low-level libraw data structures to create a nice Python interface that is easy to understand.

libraw itself provides other functions as well which I think don't belong there, and these are around all the EXIF metadata. Since reading EXIF data from image files is independent of whether the file is a RAW image or not, this should not go into rawpy. There are already many [EXIF libraries for Python](https://pypi.python.org/pypi?%3Aaction=search&term=exif&submit=search), so if there is any data not exposed via those libraries, then they should be extended instead. Note that some libraries like [ExifRead](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/ExifRead) have a description of "Read Exif metadata from tiff and jpeg files" but in fact they just depend on the EXIF format itself and can also read RAW camera images. 

Now, removing all the EXIF data from your PR that leaves us with the `.color` property (`rawpy.ImageColor (cam_mul, pre_mul, cblack, black, cmatrix, cam_xyz, profile)`) and the `.idata` property (`rawpy.ImageIData (make, model, raw_count, dng_version, is_foveon, colors, filters, xtrans, cdesc)`).

Let's look at each data item separately:

## `ImageColor` (`.color`)
`cam_mul`: This is in fact already available via the `.camera_whitebalance` property.
`pre_mul`: Same here, via the `.daylight_whitebalance` property.
`cblack` and `black`: This is combined as `.black_level_per_channel`. `black` is added to each `cblack` value and a list is returned. If there is a need to have `cblack` and `black` separately, let me know.
`cmatrix`: Already exposed via `.color_matrix`.
`cam_xyz`: Already exposed via `.rgb_xyz_matrix`
`profile`: Not available yet.

## `ImageIData ` (`.idata`)
`make` and `model`: Not available yet, but sounds like EXIF metadata.
`raw_count`: Not available yet. If this is the number of RAW pixels, then this is equivalent to `raw.raw_image.size`
`dng_version`: Not available yet, but sounds like EXIF metadata.
`is_foveon`: Already exposed as `.raw_type`.
`colors`: Already exposed as `.num_colors`.
`filters`: This describes the Bayer matrix pattern as a single integer. Already exposed in a more useful way as `.raw_pattern`.
`xtrans`: Not available yet.
`cdesc`: Already exposed as `.color_desc`.

That leaves us with `profile` and `xtrans`, assuming my understanding above is correct.

In the [libraw API docs](https://www.libraw.org/docs/API-datastruct-eng.html) it says:

> char xtrans[6][6];
char xtrans_abs[6][6];
    These matrices contains Fuji X-Trans row/col to color mapping. First one is relative to visible area, while second is positioned relative to sensor edges.

I have to admit I have no idea what this is good for. Do you have a use case for that?

> void *profile;
    Pointer to the retrieved ICC profile (if it is present in the RAW file).

It turns out that ICC profiles are also part of EXIF, so there is no need to make it available here.

In general (for any open source project) I would advise before doing such undertakings always to open an issue first to see whether things make sense and if there is an actual need.

If you need the `xtrans` information, feel free to do a bit more research on that and check whether this is part of EXIF or not. If it's not, and if it's helpful in RAW image processing (ideally with a concrete example where it's used) then this would be a good candidate for a PR.

Hi Rob, I'm aware of the OpenLDAP client configuration methods. I didn't include it in the ldap3 library because for what I know this is not defined in any RFC and applies only  to Linux/Mac and other flavors of Unix. While developing ldap3 I strictly followed the  RFCS with really few exceptions. Now that the library is quite stable I'm working on a new abstraction layer, fully read and write capable that hope to release soon in version 2 of ldap3 that includes also some minor not backward compatible functionality updates. It could be a good idea to include your PR in the new version. Probably it should not be active by default but activated by some configuration parameter to avoid issues with people not aware of this configuration functionality.

Feel free to send the PR in dev, it should work even if I'm committing many changes in the ldap3.abstract namespace.

Giovanni

Original comment by: Tarek Ziadé

If the system keyring is GNOME Keyring, you can use [SecretStorage](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/SecretStorage) module (which is usually the default Python-Keyring backend on GNOME).

For example:

    :::python3
    import secretstorage
    bus = secretstorage.dbus_init()
    collection = secretstorage.get_default_collection()
    for item in collection.get_all_items():
        print(item.get_label())
        print(item.get_attributes())

Instead of using `get_all_items()` you can also specify a filter using `search_items()` method. See [the documentation](http://pythonhosted.org/SecretStorage/) for details.

Also, you can use GNOME’s libgnome-keyring (the legacy library) and libsecret (the new library) in Python via GObject introspection.

The `concat` function isn't meant to remove falsey (or `None`) values. This mirrors the implementation in Lodash:

```node
> require('lodash').concat(null, [1]);
[ null, 1 ]
```

If you want to remove all falsey values, then you can do:

```python
>>> pydash.compact(pydash.concat(None, [1], False))
[1]
```

If you only want to remove `None` values then:

```python
>>> pydash.filter_(pydash.concat(None, [1], False), lambda item: item is not None)
[1, False]
```

I think I've found the source of the problem:
- The pycurl module uses the ``libcurl`` library that's shipped with Mac. Of course, this ``libcurl`` library is linked with the ``libldap`` that can be found on the OS by default.
- On the other side, bonsai uses a newer version of ``libldap`` which is distributed with the module itself.

So when importing both modules, the interpreter loads two different versions of the ``libldap`` library causing this weird assertion failure that you've experienced.

To prove my theory I built ``curl`` on a Mac without LDAP/LDAPS support, built and installed the pycurl module (linked with my LDAPless ``libcurl`` library) then I was able to import both pycurl and bonsai and ran the example.

But unfortunately, I don't have a fix that can solve this. The best solution that I read so far was rebuilding the ``libcurl`` to also use the newer ``libldap`` library. I haven't come across any other solution that can solve this somehow with code or configuration.

Some notes:
* Travis CI
 * I will look in to adding support for Travis CI if it will support my network dependent tests.
 * Your requirements.txt won't work for Python 3.3+ since the built-in ipaddress library is used for 3.3+ rather than ipaddr. That can be fixed by having a requirements folder with python2.txt and python3.txt.
* Logging
 * I initially intended this library to be similar to built-in libraries, using just exceptions. Due to the growth of this project and the unpredicatable output variations from the RIRs, I am strongly considering adding logging support.
 * If I do decide to go down this path, I will want it to be as verbose as possible, which could impact performance. This means using logging.Logger.isEnabledFor checks for more expensive logging operations when not at DEBUG level.
* Static json tests
 * I prefer not to run tests this way. This library is dependant on the output of the RIRs, which are still getting updated/changed occasionally. My tests which rely on actual output help identify any changes to the RIR formatting.
 * I could possibly include both online and offline tests.
* ipwhois.py splitting to multiple files
 * This sounds like a good idea, one .py file for each RIR. This will likely happen since I am also adding in JPNIC and KRNIC support per https://github.com/secynic/ipwhois/issues/67
* Parsing additional information
 * This is on my analysis/testing list. I need to figure out a good structure that will work for output from all of the RIRs, not just RIPE. This includes some ideas proposed in this pull request: https://github.com/secynic/ipwhois/pull/66

Calling `handle()` does not spawn threads.

But the thing about threads as related to [that comment](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/issues/55#issuecomment-212415652) is that, in a traditional web server, a thread may be spawned to handle each HTTP request. Since `handle()` by itself is not thread-safe, calling it directly as suggested by that comment is not safe *generally* (because many threads may be calling it at the same time). But *in the context of Flask*, it is safe because the Flask webserver is single-threaded (because it is only for testing, not for deployment). I hope this makes it clear.

A polynomial function is one that can be written in the form:

    f(x) = a_n x^n + a_n−1 x^(n−1) + . . . + a_2 x^2 + a_1 x + a_0

Where ``a_0`` through ``a_n`` are constants.

The cubic Bezier curve function looks like this:

    f(x) = A(1 - x)^3 + 3B(1 - x)^2 x + 3C(1 - x) x^2 + Dx^3

Where ``A``, ``B``, ``C`` and ``D`` are the control points and, for the purpose of evaluating an instance of the Bezier curve, are constants.  Multiplying everything out and collecting terms yields the expanded polynomial form:

    f(x) = (-A + 3B -3C + D)x^3 + (3A - 6B + 3B)x^2 + (-3A + 3B)x + A

If we say:

    a = -A + 3B - 3C + D
    b = 3A - 6B + 3B
    c = -3A + 3B
    d = A

Then we have the expanded polynomal:

    f(x) = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d

Whos indefinite integral is:

    a/4 x^4 + b/3 x^3 + c/2 x^2 + dx + E

Where ``E`` is a new constant introduced by integration.

The indefinite integral of the quadratic Bezier curve is:

    (-A + 3B - 3C + D)/4 x^4 + (3A - 6B + 3B)/3 x^3 + (-3A + 3B)/2 x^2 + Ax + E

So it is possible to integrate a cubic Bezier but this gives you the area under the curve not the length of the curve.  See: http://math.stackexchange.com/questions/922098/arc-length-of-general-polynomial

S.c. "root signatures" — `from pyroute2 import <symbol>` — are made for the external usage, so the users can rely on a permanent module signature, that doesn't change when the module layout changes. They are implemented as re-export of ordinary modules from `pyroute2/__init__.py`.

But inside the library we have to avoid using them 'cause of possible import loops. So in that case we may do that:
```python
# file pyroute2/netlink/rtnl/tcmsg/em_ipset.py

# "root import" — wrong: from pyroute2 import IPSet
from pyroute2.ipset import IPSet  # correct — fully-qualified import
```

If you're going to open additional netlink sockets from within the `IPRoute` calls, please make sure that all the temporary sockets get closed even in the case of an exception — to avoid fd leaks. All the netlink socket objects must support the context manager protocol, so use `with IPSet(…) as …: …`.

Ah, thank you! Two issues:

You cannot share producer instances across processes, only threads. I expect that is why the master process pattern is failing.

Second, producer.send() is async but is not guaranteed to deliver if you close the producer abruptly. In your final example I suspect that your producer instances are so short-lived that they are being reaped before flushing all pending messages. To guarantee delivery (or exception) call producer.send().get(timeout) or producer.flush() . otherwise you'll need to figure out how to get a producer instance per-uwsgi-thread and have it shared across requests (you would still want to flush before thread shutdown to guarantee no messages are dropped)

@python-programmer Thank you very much. There still is a lot of work I need to do to clean up the usage and documentation. And, I have some plans on how to make certain things more extendable for the developer.

As for your question ... you need to define a "handler" in the settings: `app.config.SANIC_JWT_HANDLER_PAYLOAD_SCOPES`

> Purpose: A handler method used to add scopes into a payload. It is a convenience method so that you do not need to extend the payload with the more verbose (yet, more flexible) SANIC_JWT_HANDLER_PAYLOAD_EXTEND. It should return either a string or a list of strings that meet the scope requirements. See the secion on Scopes for more details. Also, to make it easier for the developer, the user instance that is returned by the authenticate method is passed in as a parameter as seen below.

Say you have `app/lib/auth/handlers.py`. Inside you would have a method like this:

```python
async def my_scope_extender(user, *args, **kwargs):
    return user.scopes
```

And then, you would wire it up by adding a setting

```python
SANIC_JWT_HANDLER_PAYLOAD_SCOPES = 'app/lib/auth/handlers.my_scope_extender'
```

Given your example, I would expect your `User` model to have a property that can return a list of strings, with each string being a different [scope](https://github.com/ahopkins/sanic-jwt#scopes).

Let me know if this helps.

# Create your new operation by inheriting from the Operation superclass:
from Augmentor.Operations import Operation
class TranslateImage(Operation):
    # Here you can accept as many custom parameters as required:
    def __init__(self, probability,max_left_translation,max_right_translation,max_down_translation,max_up_translation):
        # Call the superclass's constructor (meaning you must
        # supply a probability value):
        Operation.__init__(self, probability)
        # Set your custom operation's member variables here as required:
        self.max_left_translation = -abs(max_left_translation)
        self.max_right_translation = max_right_translation
        self.max_down_translation = -abs(max_down_translation)
        self.max_up_translation = max_up_translation

    # Your class must implement the perform_operation method:
    def perform_operation(self, images):
        # Start of code to perform custom image operation.
        def do(image):
            random_left = random.randint(self.max_left_translation, 0)
            random_right = random.randint(0, self.max_right_translation)
            left_or_right = random.randint(0, 1)
            translation_left_right = 0
            if left_or_right == 0:
                translation_left_right= random_left
            elif left_or_right == 1:
                translation_left_right = random_right
            
            random_down = random.randint(self.max_down_translation, 0)
            random_up = random.randint(0, self.max_up_translation)
            up_or_down = random.randint(0, 1)
            translation_up_down = 0
            if up_or_down == 0:
                translation_up_down = random_up
            elif up_or_down == 1:
                translation_up_down = random_down
            a = 1
            b = 0
            c = translation_left_right #left/right (i.e. 5/-5)
            d = 0
            e = 1
            f = translation_up_down  #up/down (i.e. 5/-5)
            #image = PIL.Image.fromarray(image)
            image = image.transform(image.size, PIL.Image.AFFINE, (a, b, c, d, e, f))
            return image
        
        augmented_images = []

        for image in images:
            augmented_images.append(do(image))

        return augmented_images

It becomes difficult to follow, because you're going through a lot of different types here. 
* `file_content` is a string

* `len("".join(file_content))` is an int
* `"%s % len("".join(file_content))` is a string again
* and then your actual problem comes from `sys.stdout.write('stuff')` which returns `None` in Python2 and an int (the number of characters written) in Python3. Neither looks to be what you want.

Creating one of these and getting a commit on it is the important first step in getting a new feature merged. This is where the API and calling protocol are decided. Those are critically important decisions because they cannot be changed once released, whereas all the implementation details can be changed as needed.

Generally, best practice is to start with the analysis page (also called an feature proposal), _before_ starting coding, given that the decisions made there often have a profound effect on the implementation. Pyautogui uses screencaps to find the location of stuff on the screen. The problem on macs with a retina display is that the screen output have double the number of pixels, to take advantage of the system’s greater pixel density for a crisper image.Therefore, when you take a screenshot, you get an image containing double the number of pixels than the same image from a non-retina Mac. 
[source](http://www.macissues.com/2014/03/29/adjust-retina-macbook-screenshots-to-be-actual-size/)

One work around is to change the yield of pyscreeze._locateAll_opencv and  pyscreeze._locateAll_python (the pyautogui source code) if using a retina display:

pyscreeze._locateAll_opencv:
change
`yield (x, y, needleWidth, needleHeight)` to
`if subprocess.call("system_profiler SPDisplaysDataType | grep -i 'retina'", shell= True) == 0: 
      yield (int((matchx + region[0])*0.5), int((y + region[1])*0.5), int((needleWidth, needleHeight))*0.5)
  else:
      yield (matchx + region[0], y + region[1], needleWidth, needleHeight)`

pyscreeze._locateAll_python:
change
`yield (matchx + region[0], y + region[1], needleWidth, needleHeight)`
to
`if subprocess.call("system_profiler SPDisplaysDataType | grep -i 'retina'", shell= True) == 0:
                    yield (int((matchx + region[0])*0.5), int((y + region[1])*0.5), int((needleWidth, needleHeight))*0.5)
                else:
                    yield (matchx + region[0], y + region[1], needleWidth, needleHeight)`

As for reintroducing the `shortner` parameter to Twitter, my thinking is that this should remain up to the end user. My reasoning goes like this:

1. You're already constructing an object to attach to the Api; it's not that far from there to apply the code above to shorten any URLs in the status you're going to be posting to be passed to `PostUpdate()`.
2. Shortening a URL is a business decision (primarily), so your business logic should control and I don't think it's the place of the Api to determine how and when that happens, even if you're able to control which service is used.
3. I think it's prudent to consider that not all URLs should get shortened, but with an Api-wide shortener, this isn't really an option: you have to make a choice on instantiation and then you can't really change it. (Do you shorten all URLs or only those you control? Just breaking news from your site or from your competitors as well? What about longform where link-rot could play a factor and you might not want to rely on an external service?)
4. The logic of no. 3 gets even weirder when(/if) Twitter/Google rolls out their version of Instant Articles and then that's even more of a mess since, depending on the implementation, you're going to be dealing a whole new class of decisions as to whether something should get shortened or not.
5. Shortening  some URLs breaks the UI of modern Twitter clients. For example: if you shorten *all* URLs, then you're going to be shortening quote-replies, which are, if not ubiquitous, really popular; having an Api-wide shortener that you can't easily break out of would shorten URLs like `https://twitter.com/jack/status/129301823` and the quote-reply wouldn't work. There's no way that I can see (without hard-coding exceptions) to avoid this situation.

Anyway, those are just my thoughts on the subject, but I thought I should address it, though I don't want to speak for @bear or anybody else. I guess my preference would have this as something in that should go in the documentation so there's a reference and how-to and then remove the parameter from the API.

Thanks for sending your unit test files; using a resolution of 1024 makes it easy to place a time signature change exactly on a beat which demonstrates this problem.  Here's a simple example:

```Python

resolution = 1024
# Instantiate a MIDI Pattern (contains a list of tracks)
pattern = midi.Pattern(resolution=resolution)
# Instantiate a MIDI Track (contains a list of MIDI events)
track = midi.Track()
# Append the track to the pattern
pattern.append(track)

# Tempo 120 at 0s
tempo = midi.SetTempoEvent(tick=0)
tempo.set_bpm(120)
track.append(tempo)

# Time signature 4/4 at 0s
time = midi.TimeSignatureEvent(tick=0)
time.set_numerator(4)
time.set_denominator(4)
track.append(time)

# Fake time signature change resolution*4
time = midi.TimeSignatureEvent(tick=resolution*4)
time.set_numerator(4)
time.set_denominator(4)
track.append(time)

# Add a long note event to force pretty_midi to report lots of beats
on = midi.NoteOnEvent(tick=100, velocity=20, pitch=midi.G_3)
track.append(on)
off = midi.NoteOffEvent(tick=1000, pitch=midi.G_3)
track.append(off)

# Save the MIDI file
midi.write_midifile("example.mid", pattern)

pm = pretty_midi.PrettyMIDI('example.mid')
print pm.get_beats()
```

yields `[ 0.   0.5  1.   1.5  2.   2.   2.5]`.  I think the `isclose` solution is good, and I will add a note about how `get_beats` can still produce beats which are pretty close together in some cases.  We can leave it to the user to filter those out if they desire.

Hmm, I'm not quite certain if this is something we want to bake directly into `schema.validate` with no option to disable, since it's entirely possible someone may **want** an empty response from a request as defined behaviour (although this is unlikely).

That being said, I think it would be best to add a boolean keyword argument here like `on_empty_404=False` for example. Then if you wanted to use it by default, you could just create a partial in your application like so, and use it:

```python
import functools
validate = functools.partial(schema.validate, on_empty_404=True)
```

Hi @jamesstidard, thanks for creating this issue.

There's been a similar discussion on this issue tracker: #11.

The advanced operators (such as `concat`, `flatten` and `switch`) need the kind of logic you describe because they all deal with asynchronous sequence of sequences. So I abstracted the logic of adding and removing streamers on the fly in an asynchronous context manager called [StreamerManager](https://github.com/vxgmichel/aiostream/blob/64225e2a6bb665b5fe382eb892d8b827efb9248e/aiostream/manager.py#L13).

In your example, it would look like this:
```python
async with StreamerManager() as manager:
    for task in [go(), go(), go()]:
        await manager.enter(task)
    async for streamer, getter in manager.completed():
        try:
            result = getter()
        except StopAsyncIteration:
            pass
        else:
            if result == 50:
                await manager.enter(go())
            print(result)
            manager.schedule(streamer)
```

However, the reason why this helper is not exposed (nor documented) is because it doesn't fit the philosophy behind aiostream (i.e, building pipelines of functional operators). So feel free to use it but keep it mind that it might be changed or removed in the future.

Note that it's also possible to implement the pattern in your example using aiostream operators and an asyncio queue as feedback channel:

```python
queue = asyncio.Queue()
await queue.put(go())
await queue.put(go())
await queue.put(go())

await (stream.repeat(())
       | pipe.starmap(queue.get)
       | pipe.flatten()
       | pipe.print()
       | pipe.filter(lambda x: x == 50)
       | pipe.map(lambda _: go())
       | pipe.map(queue.put))
```

The `_get_shared_string_index()` method in SharedStrings isn't thread safe. The other methods in that class should be since they are only called from Workbook (for which there should only be one unique instance per xlsxwriter object) or via the Workbook destructor (when the worksheet threads should be `joined` and activity stopped).

> i've discovered that if i use in_memory option XlsxWriter will use SharedStrings

The SharedStrings class can be called regardless of whether you use `in_memory` or not. The `in_memory` option relates to the use of temporary files and not SharedStrings ([see the docs](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/workbook.html#constructor)).

If you wish to avoid using SharedStrings, and thus avoid locking, you can use the `constant_memory` mode. There are some caveats to that though: data needs to be written in row-column order and you can't use `add_table()` or `merge_range()`. See the Contructor docs (link above) and also [Working with Memory and Performance](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/working_with_memory.html#memory-perf).

> And is there anything else in XlsxWriter that i need to worry about?

Possibly, but nothing that springs to mind.

I believe the issue is this in util.py:

https://github.com/kurtmckee/feedparser/blob/8c6294042b749544ad87f73cf55da143bf55e921/feedparser/util.py#L91

```python
    if isinstance(realkey, list):
        for k in realkey:
            if dict.__contains__(self, k):
                return dict.__getitem__(self, k)
    elif dict.__contains__(self, realkey):
        return dict.__getitem__(self, realkey)
```

What this is doing, is checking this keymap when accessing an element.  The problem is, it is looking at *alternative* names before looking at the name *given* to the ```FeedParserDict```.  

```python
 keymap = {'channel': 'feed',
              'items': 'entries',
              'guid': 'id',
              'date': 'updated',
              'date_parsed': 'updated_parsed',
              'description': ['summary', 'subtitle'],
              'description_detail': ['summary_detail', 'subtitle_detail'],
              'url': ['href'],
              'modified': 'updated',
              'modified_parsed': 'updated_parsed',
              'issued': 'published',
              'issued_parsed': 'published_parsed',
              'copyright': 'rights',
              'copyright_detail': 'rights_detail',
              'tagline': 'subtitle',
              'tagline_detail': 'subtitle_detail'}
```


So in this case, I asked for ```feed['description']``` and it went and looked up "description" in this dictionary, saw that it had a list for ```summary``` and ```subtitle```.  Looked up both of those, and found a subtitle, so it ended up returning ```itunes:subtitle``` when I really asked for description.

That if-statement I posted above should be flipped so it **first** looks for the key I give it, and only if it doesn't find it does it go and look for alternative names.

What actually happens is this:
1. Sign in to Travis with your Github account at https://travis-ci.org/
2. Travis scans your repos. You enable for this repo, which actually adds the webhook to kick off the tests when you git push.
3. Sign in to Coveralls at https://coveralls.io/ and enable for your repo in the same manner.  It doesn't add a webhook though, because the `coveralls` command is invoked directly at the end of `.travis.yml`

The best part is integration with pull requests, because you'll see whether the tests pass or fail when someone files a PR.

Currently able to use this as a workaround:
```python
from werkzeug.http import HTTP_STATUS_CODES

# inverse lookup for status codes
iHTTP_STATUS_CODES = {error: status for status, error in HTTP_STATUS_CODES.items()}
```
The inverse status_code dictionary can be used to lookup the correct status code, and we can wrap the default error handler.
```python
@jwt_ext.jwt_error_handler
def error(error):
    code = iHTTP_STATUS_CODES.get(error.error, error.status_code)

    return {
        'error': error.error,
        'description': error.description,
        'status': code
    }, code, error.headers
```
Note, I'm currently using flask-api.  Other flask users may have more success passing the return dictionary into `jsonify()`

So it turns out that I couldn't use `set_exception` in `BasePeonyClient.request` because then this would bypass the error handle and raise all the exceptions that it encounters (the error handler is just a decorator for the `request` method).

So instead I made a new default error handler that will call `set_exception` when required. Now the error handler is a class (`ErrorHandler`) which can be inherited from to change its behavior.
You have to use `@ErrorHandler.handle` to change the behavior of the error handle when it receives an exception, the decorated method must return `ErrorHandler.RETRY` if you want to retry the request (by default it will raise the exception if the function returns None).

I'm going to close this issue. I agree we should continue exploring "dataclasses" as a possible inclusion in the stdlib. I also think that attrs made some good design decisions, and that "dataclasses" will to some extent look like a stripped down attrs.

I'll respond later to your first point.

For the inherited fields, the order is the order that the fields are defined, starting from base classes working back toward the most-derived class (so, reverse MRO).

Here's an example from the tests, which show a base class that defines x and y, then a derived class that defines z and x. The order of fields is x, y, z, but x gets its Field definition from the most-derived class.

```
        @dataclass
        class Base:
            x: str = 'base'
            y: int = 0

        @dataclass
        class C1(Base):
            z: int = 10
            x: int = 15

        o = C1()
        self.assertEqual(repr(o), 'C1(x=15,y=0,z=10)')
```

Presumably attrs can support PEP 526 too though? I view it as a benefit that attrs can support both things like PEP 526 for nicer, cleaner code OR a more verbose mechanism for older Pythons. Largely because very few of my projects that I have ever worked on can mandate ``>=$LATEST_VERSION_OF_PYTHON`` but having both means developers can use whichever best suits their purpose.

Reinventing seems to make sense when the stdlib has some unique ability that an external project doesn't have (for instance, the ability to define new language features like ``async def`` or the actual language level support for isolation in venv or the ability to define some sort of standard like logging). However when something is being done entirely in "user land", I think an obvious question is are we doing something meaningfully better, or are we just doing something different?

It feels a lot like this is just doing something different to me, which the current (proposed) feature set seems to agree with. It appears to largely be a direct copy of most/all of attrs features, just with slightly different spellings and semantics (i.e. painting the bikeshed a different color). The PEP so far tries to answer why not just using attrs, but that currently only has two points and both seem like they are anemic post-hoc rationalizations rather than real reasons.

In fact, the two reasons seem to really be the same reason, which is the implication that by supporting older versions of Python, attrs is somehow worse off than if it could depend on newer language features. While it is true that attrs cannot depend on these language features as part of it's own implementation, it does not appear to be the case that consumers of attrs could not use these features for a cleaner, more concise API (and @hynek has indicated that is true).

Does that mean that seeing if we can do something better than attrs is a bad thing to do? Well no. Experimentation is great and can come up with new ideas, and possibly something much better than has already existed. However, before we actually commit to something, I think the default should be to look at how we can incorporate the thing that already exists unless we have some really compelling reason why that thing is unsuitable.

from pyswagger import SwaggerApp
from pyswagger.scan import Scanner, Dispatcher

class OperationCollector(object):
    class Disp(Dispatcher): pass

    def __init__(self):
        self.cached_obj = {}   # <- a dict

Main issues for integrating into HA.
1. ~~Integrate protocol definitions from https://github.com/telldus/tellstick-server/tree/master/rf433/src/rf433~~ Let's just keep the current protocol definitions. Accept new protocols later as PRs if needed by anyone.
2. Decide / find out how to map local sensor id to server id, we probably need to add a mapping section to `configuration.yaml` that needs to be edited manually. (This can actually be optional. It would be possible for the implementation to decided the mapping itself by comparing sensor values for most sensors - at least in case there is only one sensor with same value reported by the server. Logic: 1) receive sensor reading over udp 2) check already known mappings, including user provided, if found call the update callback 3) check if there exists exactly one sensor reported by the server with same values (all values/scales), if so add to known mapping and call the update callback 4) log unknown sensor reading).

The JSON type is always `utf8mb4_bin`, and:

> Because utf8mb4_bin is a binary collation, comparison of JSON values is case sensitive.

https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/json.html

You could fix this by wrapping with `CAST(column AS CHAR)`, but that means re-implementing `__icontains` with a new lookup. For example:

```
chainz@localhost [14]> create table t (c json);
Query OK, 0 rows affected (0.03 sec)

chainz@localhost [15]> insert into t (c) values ('"test"');
Query OK, 1 row affected (0.00 sec)

chainz@localhost [16]> select * from t where c like '%tesT%';
Empty set (0.00 sec)

chainz@localhost [17]> select * from t where cast(c as char) like '%tesT%';
+--------+
| c      |
+--------+
| "test" |
+--------+
1 row in set (0.00 sec)
```

I'm using `requirements.txt` as a top-level file that lists all packages and their exact versions so that a fresh environment can be easily bootstrapped.

The `install_requires` lists package-level dependencies and often their versions are specified less strictly to avoid conflicts with other libraries that are relying on the same packages and to allow upgrades through `requirements.txt` without patching each `setup.py`.

If you have one main `setup.py` file it can play the role of a `requirements.txt` file just fine.

This is due to the presence of a carriage return in the `v2_0_skeleton_str` which the parser is unable to remove because it cannot decide whether or not this carriage return is useful data.
To prevent this issue, it is sufficient to edit the last two lines of the `v2_0_skeleton_str` definition from 
```
 93     ivorn="ivo://undefined">
 94 </voe:VOEvent>
```
to 
```
 93     ivorn="ivo://undefined">\
 94 </voe:VOEvent>
```
or even just
```
 93     ivorn="ivo://undefined"> </voe:VOEvent>
```

1171                     only_identity_in_encrypted_assertion = kwargs[
1172                         "only_identity_in_encrypted_assertion"]
1173                 print "keys: "+str(keys)
1174                 response = response.verify(keys)`

since outstanding_certs is none by default, key gets set to none by this and then does not get anything appended to it, so when `response = response.verify(keys)` is called response gets set equal to None. If you comment out that line it will work because then response is no longer written over as None.

Not really sure the implications of such a change, but wanted to provide more detail into where its breaking down.

Iiiiiii.... don't think an `import` statement is ever supposed to raise anything other than a subclass of `ImportError`, unless circumstances are particularly severe?

The Python docs state the following about `ImportError`:
_Raised when the import statement has troubles trying to load a module._
Which is the case here.

IMHO it would be appropriate to raise an `OSError`, if you had something like `usb1.load_library()` call, and that would fail when the binary is not found.
The issue here is that the error is raised _in an import statement_. From my point of view as a programmer, _I'm not doing anything_ yet.
(I mean, in the same vein, you could argue that "`usb1/__init__.py ` does not exist" is _technically_ an `OSError`, as it results from some syscall failing with `ENOTFOUND`. And yet...)

`try ... except ImportError` is the idiomatic way to guard against failing `import` statements. By raising something else, you're pushing knowledge and responsibility to users of the library - now I need to know that libusb, unlike the majority of other libraries, has two different things that can go wrong when importing. And that I need to code against both of them separately.

Sorry it's taken me a while to get back to this. 

There are two reasons for this, the first is that when Knox was first being put together that warning was not here in such large letters. As always technology has moved on fairly quickly.

The other is one of Platform specifics. While /dev/urandom is preferable to OpenSSL random numbers on *nix devices, there is nothing to stop someone deploying Knox on a Windows based device, where `os.random` uses the system PRNG (CryptGenRand). The specifications for this are not available, it may not be a secure generator. At the time, it was considered that using Openssl seeded with system random would be secure on all systems, without any of that messy system detection code.

It is the consideration that most often is reconsidered. I think that it might be worth putting in a setting to let this be changed per-deployment, as both options have strengths.

Hope this clarifies the decisions made.

As for your specific issue, I cannot be sure why you are always getting the same from rand.bytes. It seems to be tied to the system you are deploying to. I would be interested to see if urandom is in any way deterministic on that server, as it is meant to be used to seed OpenSSL on creation of the generator.

There is no current documentation in the package on how to run batch operations. There is a method in the batches attribute of the MailChimp class that you can use to perform this, with some basic documentation in the docstring. You would use `client.batches.execute()` and pass in a dictionary of data as mentioned [here](https://github.com/charlesthk/python-mailchimp/blob/master/mailchimp3/entities/batches.py#L36-L45). The action matching names can be found [here](https://github.com/charlesthk/python-mailchimp/blob/master/mailchimp3/helpers.py#L4-L10).

I currently have a pull request in that overhauls some of this among other things, and would require that you manually convert the body to JSON, but would allow you to simply generate the data as defined in the [endpoint documentation](http://developer.mailchimp.com/documentation/mailchimp/reference/batches/#create-post_batches) and just send it.

Thank you for the PR and I'm sorry that I couldn't merge it because I had a little bit more to be added regarding this. I also recommend that you upgrade your ODBC driver to a newer one. See #70 for more details.

I guess you're using an old `SQL Server` (WDAC) driver which treats `nvarchar` as `ntext` and that's why you're experiencing the issue because there's no reference to `ntext` type inside django-pyodbc-azure. See the following document for more details about the difference between an old WDAC driver and modern drivers for SQL Server.
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational-databases/native-client/applications/updating-an-application-to-sql-server-native-client-from-mdac

I strongly recommend that you install Microsoft ODBC Driver for SQL Server on your computer and utilize it from your Django app by specifying its name as the `driver` option in `DATABASES` settings.
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/connect/odbc/windows/microsoft-odbc-driver-for-sql-server-on-windows

As it currently stands this obviously wont work with sanic as is, but I imagine it could be ported over. Most of the code in here doesn't do any blocking, so it shouldn't be hard to get it working with asyncio (at least, as far as I understand it). We would want to look at the underlying pyJWT library to make sure there isn't anything potentially blocking in there, but I doubt there would be.

The one except to this would be the token blacklisting functionality in this extension. This uses simplekv to access an underlying store (redis, memcache, sqlalchemy, etc), and afaik that does block and doesn't have an non-blocking/asyncio api that could be used. If you dropped the token blacklist feature, I think this would get much easier to port over. I'm not sure what exactly would need to be done if that feature were to be kept, but if I had to guess I think the easiest thing might be to only allow one of the stores to be allowed (for example redis) and utilize an existing asyncio library for interacting with it.

The last thing would be the actual interfacing with sanic. Stuff like ```init_app`` (if they have that), or storing data on whatever their equivalent of the application context / flask.g is so that it can be accessed later in the endpoints. I wouldn't expect that to be too difficult, but I haven't used sanic so I couldn't say for sure.

At present I'm not using sanic myself, and am pretty strapped for time, so I couldn't really port this over. But if someone wanted to fork this and try to get it ported over, I would be more then happy to work with them in getting everything up and running, plus making sure improvements from this library make its way over there, and vice versa.

Cheers.

I can guarantee others know more about the the history of the project, but I can give my own history of why I chose to use this version (and subsequently ended up helping maintain it).

In short, the original django-registration written by ubernostrum was abandoned (for lack of a better word). Since there was still interest in the package it was forked to this repository and work continued. Up until fairy recently, this version was the only actively developed version. Recently, ubernostrum began working on the original version again.

I can't speak to ubernostrum's current version, but this version is active and adopted by many people. However, I will say that if you are looking to adopt a package to help with user registration in Django I would highly recommend considering django-allauth: https://github.com/pennersr/django-allauth

Hello,

As it's running in Celery, I assume it is running in parallel to the code creating the object?
Hvad proceeds in the following way:

1) Save the master object
2) If some translated fields are to be saved, then save the translation object.

This is not run in a transaction by default, so user code can choose whether it wants one, or whether it wants handle the cases in which the translation has not been saved yet gracefully.

So it would look like the celery process is awaken and starts handling the new object before `hvad` has completed the save.

As a sidenote, depending on which fields are saved (using the `update_fields` argument of `save()`), `hvad` will intelligently save either `MyTranslatableModel`, `MyTranslatableModel._meta.translations_model` or both. So you might want to have both trigger your celery code.

In this case, creating a new object will trigger two events, one for “new object created” and one for “new translation created”.

```python
import yaml
from bravado_core.spec import Spec
with open(...) as f:
    spec_dict = yaml.load(f)
spec = Spec.from_dict(spec_dict)
```

In this case if your responseObject has no quote around ``200`` we cannot do anything fancy because ``yaml.load(f)`` reads it as an integer instead of string (expected type) and so the validation will fail.

Swagger specs example are available on [Swagger Spec - Response Object](http://swagger.io/specification/#responseObject)

```yaml
    ....
    response:
        200:
            description: Dummy Response
    ....
```

I understand why this would be difficult, and I'll close the ticket and resign to using the above solution for developing local modules.

I'll attempt to explain the process behind npm link, with my use case as an example.

I am developing a module called react-native-waterfall, it implements a generic waterfall view.
I am at the same time developing, in a sister folder, a module called react-native-social-waterfall, and this module depends on the above module.
I am at the same time developing utility modules, which both of the above modules depend on.

npm requires modules from either: 

1. a globally qualified name e.g react-native-waterfall
2. a path e.g ../react-native-waterfall
3. an url e.g https://github.com/facebook/watchman/

The second solution has 2 problems:
1. Every time you change any file in a dependent module, you have to run npm install ../react-native-waterfall to reinstall the module.
2.  You are checking in code that doesn't mirror the final product, where you will be using globally qualified namespaces hosted on npm.

The third solution has 2 problems as well(both are slow)
1. Every time you change any file in a dependent module, you have to commit it and push it to origin.
2. You must then go back to your dependent module and do npm update to grab the latest commit

npm link works the following way:
1. You go into the depended upon module and write npm link, this will register react-native-waterfall as a globally qualified name
2. You go into the dependent module and write npm link react-native-waterfall, this will register a symlink to the globally qualified name, using the folder where you wrote npm link.

This allows the following benefits:
1. Your checked in code mirrors the final product.
2. You do not have to do anything to update the local dependency files, meaning you can quickly test code without committing / running commands.
3. You can even edit the depended upon module in your dependent folder, and the changes will be applied to the original module.

I've updated this PR to reflect the last proposal - a class always shows its configurable traits. The help is truncated to the first line plus "See also" if its parent class is also in the config file.

The implicit inclusion of all parent classes is also removed, because it is no longer needed to ensure that all the config options show up somewhere. The implicit behavior effectively changed the meaning of the `classes` list from the list of classes that should be considered for inclusion to the list of classes to walk, including all of their parents, making it impossible for authors to request that a parent class be excluded from config.

The behavior is now much closer to the current release behavior, with the exception that redundant help output is truncated.

Billiard **Pool** uses nested class **Process**  to add boolean attribute __controlled_termination_ to process class. ### Solution for my problem
The *isSimpleSearch* method in `utils.py` returns `True` for the following condition
```python
if bool(simpleCharacters.match(term)):
    return True
```
Um auch Queries wie 'Dipl.-Inf. Christian Schneider' als SimpleSearch zu behandeln muss die regular expression `simpleCharacters`
Since we want to process queries like 'Dipl.-Inf. Jon Doe' normally, i.e. using the search pattern we defined in collective.solr, we had to change the following line in `utils.py`
```python
simpleCharacters = compile(r'^[\w\d\?\*\s]+$', UNICODE)
```
by adding the regex for '.' and '-':
```python
simpleCharacters = compile(r'^[\w\d\?\-\.\*\s]+$', UNICODE)
```
This solved my problem. Question to the developer, is there an easier way or is this hotfix really necessary?

This fails because venv does not have `activate_this.py`, which Pipenv uses to find the target command, and initialise the environment for the subprocess to be run. [Vinay (the author of venv) isn’t keen on adding it, however.](https://bugs.python.org/issue21496) The good news is, `activate_this.py` actually works perfectly for venvs, so the simplest way to fix this is to find a virtualenv, and copy the file into your venv.

Venv is not (yet?) supported by Pipenv at the moment, so it is probably not the time to solve this. A couple notes on this topic for future reference:

* CPython contains some integration code to venv, so we does not need to do much of what `activate_this.py` does. Most things “work” directly without any configuration (CPython is smart enough to handle them).
* What we *do* need to do is to implement the logic to find the correct executable (i.e. manipulate `PATH`). The logic is already there (the `which` function), but we need to change the implementation, and still maintain compatibility to virtualenv. It would likely be best to implement this as separate backends of `run` for venv and virtualenv, like what we already do for Windows and POSIX.

There is no correctness either way here. Pipenv using virtualenv is only an implementation detail. There has already been efforts to push for a venv backend which eventually died out due to lack of interest, but you are welcomed to revive it), and it is totally possible for Pipenv to move to venv eventually. No matter what the situation is, Pipenv does not make a distinction between virtualenv and venv libraries, and users know only they are using “a virtual environment”, not virtualenv or venv. The “venv” option is simply a shorthand of “virtual environment”.

I have yet to see any confusion from the name Changing it would be a purely theoretical decision, not practical, and would require another renaming when Pipenv (eventually, after it gains enough adoption for people to contribute) moves to venv, which is practically worse than just keeping the (already okay) naming.

Since 15.1.0 release TxMongo supports Write-Concern options:

- For old-style query methods write-concern options can be passed as named args: `coll.insert(..., w=1, j=True)`. Specified write-concern options will be passed to `getLastError`.
- For new-style query methods (`*_one`, `*_many`) write-concern options can be set using `Collection.with_options` method: `coll.with_options(write_concern=WriteConcern(w=2, j=True)).insert_one(...)`. Write-concern options will be passed to corresponding MongoDB query command.

I can't say I'm a fan of this.  I think it complicates implementations of bolts for the basic case. `process` --> `process_default` will make no sense for most users since most users don't use streams.  It's not awful, but it requires new users to understand a fairly advanced topic if they want to know why the function is named as such.  The current way to do this would be like this, which I don't find onerous at all:

```python
def process(self, tup):
    if tup.stream == 'foo':
        self._process_foo(tup)
    elif tup.stream == 'bar':
        self._process_bar(tup)
    else:
        self._process_default(tup)
```

and even that can be shortened by putting all the functions in a dict and using that like a switch statement.

```python
def process(self, tup):
    process_fn = self._process_fns.get(tup.stream, self._process_default)
    process_fn(tup)
```

It's a few more lines for the user to write, but `process_default` smells strongly of over-engineering to me.

Summary of changes discussed at SciPy sprints with @jni and @stefanv 

* Home in `morphology`, exposed also in `segmentation`
* Split function into two to avoid conditionally branching output, second is `flood_fill_mask`
* Revise output to return the mask rather than indices; encourage users to convert mask to indices if they have memory concerns on repeated runs.  This is the fastest and most Pythonic in the vein of 'one best way'.
* Both function signatures pruned, now cleaner
* Revised helper functions as needed to allow either C- or Fortran contiguous memory.

> how can you possibly end up with a messed up system?

Very easily.  For example, by running `python setup.py develop` at any point in the future, which is a near certainty for someone actively working in both projects.

Expecting the user to always start from a clean system is at odds with an incremental build system that is supposed to support in-place editing.  We might as well not allow it at all and instead remove support for the `python setup.py develop` workflow.

> If any base class is undefined, SWIG still generates correct type relationships. For instance, a function accepting a Foo * will accept any object derived from Foo regardless of whether or not SWIG actually wrapped the Foo class. If you really don't want to generate wrappers for the base class, but you want to silence the warning, you might consider using the %import directive to include the file that defines Foo. %import simply gathers type information, but doesn't generate wrappers.* Alternatively, you could just define Foo as an empty class in the SWIG interface or use warning suppression.

Yes yes, dynamic set were not meant as the only available sets. But even with static sets the question remains: in order to properly expose the fact that a record now entered a set (e.g. because the set definition has been amended) or it leaves it, we have to either:
* don't care about it
* keep track of all the timestamps of all the `N` records `x` `M` sets defined.

* no longer do it
* move it to a dedicated Model that would implement such matrix and that a new daemon would keep up-to-date.

Interfaces specify how the object is *used*, not how it is implemented. That is, a method in an interface is from the caller's perspective, so you only write the arguments that a caller would supply. (Remember that interfaces can be implemented by more than just classes, they can be implemented by things like modules.) So what this means is that you pretty much *never* need to put `self` in an argument list in an interface. The `self` may be there in the *implementation* if you use a class, but the caller doesn't need to know that or supply it (it's handled magically by Python).

If you take `self` out, I believe you'll see better results.

Basically it's just copied from R (who copied it from S), yeah, and in R/S it's important to have a special character because that character is the built-in operator that switches on formula parsing mode. That doesn't apply to us. Technically supporting `=` in patsy would be trivial, it wouldn't break anything.

OTOH I do kind of like that `~` emphasizes that this is not actually a numerical equation, like `y = x1 + x2` looks like you're setting `y` to be the sum of `x1` and `x2`, which would be a pretty misleading interpretation?

I've also played around with the idea of using `=` to enable a stricter interpretation mode, that disables automatic intercepts and automatic redundancy removal, since these seem to be sticking points for some people.

Given that `~` works perfectly well, and "there should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it", and the above, I'm inclined to play it safe and stick with just `~` for now. We can revisit this later if there's demand though...

One of reasons might be the fact that the first 9 bits of float ( https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single-precision_floating-point_format ) is sign and exponent, which should be nearly the same for most of points. Also the next 8 bits: the most significant of the fraction, should be similar for neighboring points.
So if you group even or odd positions, you make these similar bytes closer and so simpler for compressor to use their similarity (most of them work on bytes).

But generally, these formats seem extremely wasteful - you could probably get an order of magnitude better compression with a cheap but dedicated compressor.
Starting with storing differences instead of absolute positions, additionally entropy coder coder should be used for the most significant digits.
Storing the mesh is a graph compression problem and mesh is a very specific one - should be cheaply compressible.
My first thought is to cover all triangles with a tree (like in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Icosaedro_desarrollo.gif ) - then go through the tree and add new information for each node: usually one vertex per node - just store the difference of its position to the previous ones. 
Or even less as it has to agree with neighbors - one distance per node (difference from equilateral triangle) seems sufficient.

@gromgull - let me know if we should move the discussion to #649, but i'll respond here for now!

this hack does fix #632 as the cache would contain the full URI.

i completely agree that this is a hack and should be replaced with sth better. however, if you do move the triples and bind the namespace there, you would regenerate the cache. if you simply copied the namespaces internally without going through a `bind` then you would not have the new cache. 

the key here is that the cache generation happens on bind, and that's the point. the namespace prefix is a serialization concept not an RDF concept. RDF simply says URIs. turtle/trig on the other hand allows prefix-es.

therefore, if i explicitly bind a prefix to a valid string, rdflib shouldn't parse it down to a prefix, URI, and part. so both of the following prefixes would be completely valid:

```turtle
@prefix ex: <http://example.org/> .
@prefix ex_foo: <http://example.org/#foo>
```

the problem right now is that the prefix generator in rdflib takes a decision that the prefix cannot be: `<http://example.org/#foo>` even though it's been instructed to by the user and is in full compliance with turtle specs.

```python
import rdflib as rl
g = rl.Graph()
g.bind('ex', 'http://example.org/#foo')
g.compute_qname('http://example.org/#foo')
```

Currently, it reads in a file which is the output of Craig's Poisson solver.  Then as it builds up charge in the sensor, it assumes a linear response to the accumulated charge and moves the pixel boundaries proportionally.

I suspect this is what you call the fast interpolator?

So for LSST sensors, we could have the output of Craig's solver in the GalSim repo.  Currently, we have one of them, but I don't recall which vendor this corresponds to.  Or if there is even a substantial difference between them.

But we also wanted to let people who wanted to use this code for a different sensor be able to create this file on their own for their own detector's characteristics.  That's the bit that's not currently in the GalSim repo.

```py
import requests

A nice way to handle this is to have a `requirements-dev.txt` file that has dev-specific dependencies, like `chalice` in this case. This file can include the base requirements file so everything still gets installed with a single command. A sample would look like this:
```
chalice
-r requirements.txt
```

When someone clones your repo and wants to start developing, they run `pip install -r requirements-dev.txt`. This installs chalice and then everything inside `requirements.txt`, while keeping the dev dependencies separate from the deployment dependencies.

In general it's just a question of where you draw the "API boundary" - at the module level or at the package level. If you draw it at the package level, then what we're doing now is fine. If you draw it at the module level, then importing private members from other modules is a no-no, because you're overstepping the boundary. IMO, if a module's private function is useful outside of that module it should either be public or moved into a shared dependency.

> There is a method called _Worker._main_thread_terminated, which claims to send unsent logs. However, it does not seem to do anything other than calling print() a couple of times. It seems to me that this is a bug in the current implementation already.


The call to [`self.stop`](https://github.com/tcwalther/google-cloud-python/blob/2ed09e5aaf2bbf74d5d5ca544d2ad8f7a1de98c7/logging/google/cloud/logging/handlers/transports/background_thread.py#L219) attempts to [block](https://github.com/tcwalther/google-cloud-python/blob/2ed09e5aaf2bbf74d5d5ca544d2ad8f7a1de98c7/logging/google/cloud/logging/handlers/transports/background_thread.py#L198) until the background thread has sent pending logs.

> When reviewing this PR, I would be particularly interested in your opinion on how safe it is when a Python processes terminates due to an exception - is there a possibility for log messages getting lost?

This is always possible, yes, however, an exception on the *main* thread (or any thread other than *this* background thread) will not cause this to lose logs. Lost logs can happen due to one of three reasons:

1. The request to send the logs fails (due to timeout, etc.)
2. The main thread dies for any reason (graceful exit, exception, etc.) and sending the pending logs takes longer than `grace_period` seconds.
3. There is a bug in the background thread that causes it to terminate without sending logs.

```
import time

@Leviathan1995 
和内存相关的一个比较大的问题在 https://github.com/Leviathan1995/fari-proxy/blob/master/http/http.go#L18 这个函数里,每次发送一段数据都要先把数据转成字符串,再把字符串拼接起来,再把字符串转成slice,这里每一步都会涉及内存分配.以及每次 Encode 和 Decode 也都分配了新的内存.
还可能会有其他的问题我就没细看了.

另外每次发送数据都怼一个 HTTP 请求头这种做法我认为甚至算不上伪装,只是在折腾自己和浪费带宽.

“几乎所有 DNS 服务器都是支持 RFC1794 的”
是的，几乎每次dig www.google.com 返回的顺序都是不一样的
但是对于个人域名，返回的顺序是和域名管理后台添加的顺序是一样的，不管什么时候请求，得到的都是一个顺序，至少我自己域名是这样的
并且，在通过SS多线程下载时，SS连接服务器时随机选一个IP的下载速度明显大于只连接一个IP的。

所以我这测试的结果是随机还是又一定意义的。

I wrote the wrapper `aws-cloudformation.sh` script below as a workaround

    #!/bin/bash
    
    # wrapper around "aws cloudformation" CLI to ignore certain pseudo-errors
    
    # aws cloudformation deploy exits with 255 for "No changes to deploy" see: https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/issues/71
    # this script exits with 0 for that case
    
    STDERR=$(( aws cloudformation "$@" ) 2>&1)
    ERROR_CODE=$?
    echo ${STDERR} 1>&2
    
    if [[ "${ERROR_CODE}" -eq "255" && "${STDERR}" =~ "No changes to deploy" ]]; then exit 0; fi 
    
    exit ${ERROR_CODE}

Well, since the recent additions to styles capability in release 0.8.0, I should probably revisit that documentation passage. It does rather imply that nothing will happen when applying a non-existent style.

Your suspicion is correct. Word won't complain, but now python-docx does if you attempt to assign a style that isn't defined.

I'm not sure what style definition you're seeing for `Heading 2` in your styles.xml, but I suspect it is a *latent style definition*. You can read about those in the documentation here:
http://python-docx.readthedocs.org/en/latest/user/styles-understanding.html#latent-styles

and here:
http://python-docx.readthedocs.org/en/latest/user/styles-using.html#working-with-latent-styles

In short, they only define the UI behaviors for a built-in style, like whether it should appear in the styles gallery, when that style isn't defined in the document yet. Word will add the explicity built-in style definition to the `styles.xml` part as a `<w:style>..` element (not a `<w:lsdException>` element) the first time it is used.

In general, what you would want to do is either define `Heading 2` in your starting document if you wanted to use it in the document you're building, or define it explicitly in your code using `.add_style()` before calling `.add_heading()`.

You can add it to your starting document simply by creating a paragraph, assigning the `Heading 2` paragraph style to it, and then deleting the paragraph. The style will be retained in the file once used, even if what it was applied to is now gone.

Yes, imageio won't auto-download any library.

As for images... there is a collection of images [online](http://imageio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/standardimages.html) which will be auto-downloaded, as defined [here](https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/master/imageio/core/request.py#L33). Please don't do this! Many people use python 2.7 and have no immediate intention of changing. For example, Ubuntu 16.04 LTS (the version I run) uses python 2.7 as its default. There are significant libraries that haven't been ported to python 3.

For 2.7 support: I am not sure of removing of 2.7 support is feasible yet. I'd be open to deprecating it now, with the following implications
* we would still fix any bugs and maintain existing code and features
* we could - if effort for comatability would be too high - add new features that are 3.x only (given that they don't break at import)
* we would line up removal with the official end of maintenance in 2020 (https://pythonclock.org/)

However I think this discussion should probably go into yet another separate issue.

I would be in favor of any change that would lead to less time between releases. A fixed schedule could be one way to go. I might worry (a little), that it would be a bit too rigid, and therefore something that we might end up not complying with. It also depends on how much work is actually entailed in an release. @stefankoegl  can you comment on that, roughly how much time you use on it, varying of course with the amount of changes.

An alternative (which I'm not sure I think is better, but at least something to consider) is to set the release date to no later than 3 months from the last release. That way, if things get delayed, we wont have to worry about getting out of alignment with the release schedule, and if we want to release earlier, because of important bug fixes, we can do that as well.

I'm not sure if we wanted to add this feature if this is a simple patch. Currently we make these assumptions:

* If a user has a Coach **Role** for a Classroom, they are a Coach
* If a user has a **Membership**, they are a Learner

Having a Coach Role does not entail having a Membership, so if a Coach wants to participate as a Learner, we currently need to explicitly make them a Member.

To show Coaches classes they are only Coaches for, we would need to modify `LearnerClassroomSerializer` to treat Coach-Role-ship as a proxy for Membership (and what happens when a Coach actually is a Member?)

Ok, so I pushed a number of changes to fix the issue related to processing the rules. This means the previous rules are "gone" and replaced with the following set:

- "Network ACLs allow all ingress/egress traffic (custom/default)", which means 4 rules with the following:
  - ingress/egress is the direction
  - default meaning that these are NACLs as created by default by AWS (2 rules, 100 allow all, 32565 deny all)
  - custom meaning any NACL that does not match the default AWS rules

The distinction between custom and default is helpful to determine whether an attempt at creating NACLs was made (and errors occured) or whether the default behavior was left as is.

- "Unused network ACL": when a NACL that is not the default for the VPC is not attached to any subnet

- "Subnet with allow all ingress/egress NACLs": somewhat redundant w/ the first set of rules, but highlights subnets to which bad NACLs have been applied to.

@girder/developers I'd like to get some consensus on client controls over nvidia docker usage. As written, this code introspects the container, detects if it requires nvidia-docker support, and if so, configures the container to run with the nvidia bindings. This means any nvidia-based container will require the worker node to be running the nvidia-docker plugin in its docker engine. The question is, should we have some sort of control in the task spec to explicitly enable or disable the nvidia behavior, and what should be its default value?

We could also defer this feature until later if we don't think it's important in the first pass.

Getting a Microwave object to read in a time parameter is a brand new feature request that currently does not exist in the Thor environment. Because this would be an entirely new feature it is not currently on the top of our priority list, however we will work to implement it in the future.

It is possible to implement this yourself in Unity by creating a custom microwave script that will accept a time parameter. Depending on what you want to do exactly you can read in inputs from a text input field and pass that into the Microwave function, or use the <ServerAction> class that most of our Agent’s actions receive parameters from.

Check this for more information about text input:
https://docs.unity3d.com/Manual/script-InputField.html

Input fields read in values as strings by default, so if you wanted to convert a string input into a number, you could do something like float.Parse(<string>).

I (finally) generated and pushed the data for the pre-scipy-1.0 reproducibility tests. As discussed before in #459, the rounding changes in `scipy.ndimage.filters.uniform_filter1d` lead to minor changes in our preprocessed images. These propagate all the way into the outcome of `find`, `refine` and `characterize`. The fact that the test images are very noisy makes them susceptible to these subtle changes in preprocessing. This is a good thing, it allows us to catch these kind of changes. But it requires separate reference data for pre- and post-scipy 1.0 reproducibility tests.

Should the user need to turn on scheduling per tenant in config.py to use it, or will it be the default?

Which of the following strategies is what we want? Should we use a device from config.py instead of scheduling if it exists?

##### Prefer devices from config.py

 1. Look for a device affinity for this tenant. If there already is one use it.
 2. Look for a device this tenant can use. If there is one, use it.
 3. Look for an acos image this tenant can use. If there is one, spawn an instance of it.
 4. Fail saying that there are no devices the tenant can use and no images to launch an instance from.

##### Fall back on devices from config.py

 1. Look for a device affinity for this tenant. If there already is one use it.
 2. Look for an acos image this tenant can use. If there is one, spawn an instance of it and use it
 3. Look for a device this tenant can use. If there is one, use it.
 4. Fail saying that there are no images to launch an instance from and no devices the tenant can use,

I don't think this handles one specific case.  You _are_ checking that the addition of a wildcard domain isn't going to obstruct access to an existing application unless they have the same owner, but I believe the inverse needs to be checked as well.

Supposed `*.foo.com` already exists and is mapped to some app `foo`.  If I come along, even as a user with no permissions on the app `foo`, if I am not mistaken, as it currently stands with this PR, I could add `bar.foo.com` to an app of my own and no conflict would be detected.  Requests to `bar.foo.com` would be intercepted by my new application with the owner of `*.foo.com` being none the wiser.

    elif is_text(value):

    elif is_integer(value):

    elif is_integer(value):

With the current version (1.6.3-1) of openmpi, mpicc processes unrunable executables.

The error is due to the executable tries to look for libmpi from `@rpath/../lib` but libmpi is actually under the conda root.

However, 

1. mpi4py works fine.

2. If I prefix `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`=conda_root/lib the executable runs.

3. An extension modules that links against openmpi, works after being installed (via pip) to the conda environment. (sometimes adding `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` helps the configue scripts to run through cross compilation detection.)

I am not sufficiently experienced with path handling in macs to find a proper solution. This is not very important since we never intended to use mpicc from conda package to build an executable ;; we only use it to build python extensions which still works after they are installed to the conda env. But it's worth keeping an record of this here.
