I would like to share an update on this. The functional aspects seem OK. I'm using this version daily at work and have manually tested some of the added features. I have adapted the VHDL logging test and will add tests for the Python side before creating a PR.

(Please not that currently this only includes adding colors to the VHDL logging system, not the highlight of built in errors and warnings from simulators.)

In the meantime, I would like to share a summary of the changes so you can point issues I may have missed.

* `vunit/ui.py`:
  * `VUnit` class passes the `--gui` argument and its own printer to the `TestRunner` when running tests
* `vunit/test_runner.py`:
  * Added `CsvLogFormatter` class to format and colorize log records
  * Added 2 arguments to the `TestRunner` constructor:
    * `printer`: passed on to CsvLogFormatter objects
    * `on_gui`: used when running the test suites so the `runner_cfg` generic includes a `running on gui : True/False` field. This disables printing special characters on the transcript window
* `vunit/color_printer.py`:
  * Defines the colors used when formatting the log record
  * Log colors can be changed via `VUNIT_COLORS` environment variable
* `vunit/test_report.py`:
  * Removing control chars when generating XML at `TestResult.to_xml` method
* `vunit/vhdl/run/src/run.vhd`:
  * Uses the `running on gui` parameter to fallback to `verbose_no_colors` when running on GUI
* `vunit/vhdl/logging/src/log_formatting.vhd`:
  * The `verbose_format` procedure formats the log record into a CSV format that will be formatted by the `CsvLogFormatter` class on the Python side
* `vunit/vhdl/logging/src/log_types.vhd`:
  * Added log format `verbose_no_colors` to revert to the previous mode
* `vunit/vhdl/logging/test/tb_logging.vhd`:
  * Added tests for the CSV log format used for coloring

Please let me know if you have any suggestions.

@ericwol-msft can you review?

You can specify just storage account name with --account-name (or use the environment variable) and it will automatically attempt to get the storage key for you. However, I don't suggest that because it doesn't cache the key which means you are making multiple REST calls per command You also have to include the storage account name parameter every time. You could set the storage account name environment variable, but then you'd still incur the double REST calls and if you're going to be doing a lot of storage commands, setting the connection string makes sense.

If you are using a SAS token to authenticate, it is likely because you don't have the permissions to retrieve the key and the token was given to you.

Thanks for reporting this @johanburati .

The `telemetry_upload.py` is spawned after the command is executed to send telemetry data in the background. It occurs to me that the process fails to finish sending requests in a timely fashion cause processes accumulate. 

I assume there is an automation process/script, running a large number of CLI commands (blob uploading?) therefore accumulate this many telemetry uploading processes. Do these processes eventually finish over time? Or are they simply blocked (the later is worse, of course.) Is there any settings on your system may interfere with the `telemetry_upload.py` process?

One short-term mitigation is to use `az storage blob upload-batch` command to upload multiple blobs in one command so as to reduce the amount of telemetry to be sent. And the other option is to disable telemetry through `az confgiure` command. However, we do appreciate you enable the telemetry so that we can improve our product.

Furthermore, there is an opportunity for us to improve the telemetry mechanism to group the telemetry when a large number of commands are executed spontaneously.

The version of pip that comes with `apt-get install pip/pip3` is not recent. I got 1.5.4 which was released in 2014.
Older versions of pip did not install pre-release packages by default.

I recommend upgrading pip:
```
$ pip/pip3 install --upgrade pip
```

1. Create an UbuntuLTS VM - `az vm create -g <RG> -n <NAME> --image UbuntuLTS`

After using the defaults feature since it was released, I agree with @chrisdias. It's kind of strange to run a list command and not have it scoped to the default resource group.

I wouldn't remove the old documentation as that's a perfectly valid scenario.
With the current method (`docker run -it azuresdk/azure-cli-python:latest`), you can still achieve everything described:
- you don't have to create a new container everytime as the one initially created will still be running
- you don't have to stay in the container's shell as you can do `docker exec <container> az vm list` for example
- you don't have to login over and over as the container holds the credentials
+ You also get isolation of the persisted login info as it's in the container rather than mapped to a host directory.

I think this suggestion is neat though so we can still add documentation to use the container in this way as an alternative method. I'll work on a PR for this.

Hi @jefffall, suffice to say, this is a painful experience.  Please use this as a template:

```json
{
      "odata.type": "Microsoft.Azure.Management.Insights.Models.ThresholdRuleCondition",
      "data_source": {
        "odata.type": "Microsoft.Azure.Management.Insights.Models.RuleMetricDataSource",
        "resource_uri": "{your resource URI}",
        "metric_name": "Percentage CPU"
      },
      "operator": "GreaterThan",
      "threshold": 90,
      "window_size": "PT15M",
      "time_aggregation": "Average"
    }
```

You can consult here:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/monitor/alertrules

In the latest update to CLI 2.0 the keys can use either this_format or thisFormat, but since you probably have an older version, use this_format.

We appreciate and want to encourage contributions to the library and thank you for your patience in this case. In the future, we will try to communicate larger changes better. At the same time per the [contribution guidelines](http://azure.github.io/guidelines.html) linked to in our README, it would be helpful if before you start working on a substantial code contribution that you discuss it with the team. In this way we can communicate with you details of any changes that may affect the feature as well as contribute to your design process to ensure we can accept the resulting code.

Python 3.3 and PyPy3 are only partially supported because line numbers were removed from `AST.arg`:

```python
# Python 2.7
ipdb> ast.dump(node.args.args[0])
"Name(id='a', ctx=Param())"
ipdb> node.args.args[0].__dict__
{'col_offset': 4, 'id': 'a', 'ctx': <_ast.Param object at 0x7fbebd338190>, 'lineno': 3}

# Python 3.3
ipdb> ast.dump(node.args.args[0])
"arg(arg='a', annotation=None)"
ipdb> node.args.args[0].__dict__
{'annotation': None, 'arg': 'a'}

# Python 3.4
ipdb> ast.dump(node.args.args[0])
"arg(arg='a', annotation=None)"
ipdb> node.args.args[0].__dict__
{'lineno': 3, 'annotation': None, 'col_offset': 4, 'arg': 'a'}
```

It's also noted in [Python 3.3 docs](https://docs.python.org/3.3/library/ast.html):
> arg = (identifier arg, expr? annotation)

Compared to [Python 3.4 docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ast.html):
>     arg = (identifier arg, expr? annotation)
           attributes (int lineno, int col_offset)

The whole purpose of producing a common specification, though, is that Markdown can be interoperable, and not dependent on whatever ad-hoc extensions are implemented in particular libraries and not properly specified. So again, we should wait for CommonMark to specify some kind of syntax for extensions to use, analogous to roles and directives in rst. I know that's annoying, but if it increases the pressure on CommonMark to do that, everyone wins eventually.

We will probably never force people to switch to the new extension syntax for equations, because that would break existing content. But everything else should go through one extension syntax, even if that's a bit ugly, rather than trying to pick special symbols for each thing to make it concise.

You bring up some good points about UX design in general and for this issue specifically. In working with Cameron and the Bloomberg design team, I have watched them use two tools in making UX design decisions: 1) survey other tools and 2) observational user tests. 

On the question of researching what other "similar" UIs are doing, that is surely not the end, but it does highly constrain the solutions investigated.

You bring up good points about the target size, the usage of text+icons in other UIs and the discoverability issues of icons. The ribbon design of Microsoft was an answer to their ever growing set of toolbars in incomprehensible icons. However, We don't have that issue in the classic notebook or JupyterLab - there are a very small set of icons, and they use very standard icons.

A good comparison set would be other interactive computing UIs. Here is a quick survey of other "notebook" like UIs:

* CoCalc (traditional play button in toolbar)
* nteract (traditional play button in upper R of cell)
* DataBricks (traditional play button in toolbar)
* Zeppelin (traditional play button in upper R of cell)
* RStudio ("Run" text plus different icon in toolbar)
* Online Mathematica (no icon/button, just shift+enter and menu item)
* Colaboratory (traditional play button in the prompt area)

Based on this, it appears that our usage of the play+stop icon in the classic notebook stands out (we have gone back to standard play in JupyterLab). Some of these tools had a separate "run all" icon with text on that one (the icon was non-standard), other handled run all in a menu item.

One the observational user research side, we actually have some data about this. At JupyterCon we did a 30m observational user test ("think aloud style") on 26 users. This included a range of users (we have their biographical/experience info). There were 2 tasks where the user needed to run a cell (they were not told how). Out of 26 users, all completed the task successfully wth their first action (24 used shift+enter, 2 used the play icon). This was the JupyterLab UI with the play button in the toolbar icon - no "run" text. This user tests has sampling bias in that the individuals were at JupyterCon and all the but one had used JupyterLab or the classic notebook. But this data does support the idea that the target size of the button isn't important to optimize repetitive usage - users are relying on shift+enter once they get going.

However, having the "Run" text in the button might be useful for new users (dicoverability). My only observations on that front are in teaching university students data science and scientific computing (probably 300 over the past few years). Those users quickly learn the play button and I have to work hard to get some of them to start to use shift+enter. However, those users have a "guided" experience of the notebook - I teach them how to use it and can answer questions. For users trying out Jupyter alone, I have no data that is not purely anecdotal.

Based on these factors, this is the direction I am thinking (and which we are taking in JupyterLab):

* Stick with the familiar standard play button with no text in the toolbar button (not the play+once variant in the classic notebook currently)
* Add a play button to the upper right corner (or prompt) of each cell for quicker access.
* Make sure the buttons have a descriptive tool text.

With binder, we can more easily user test the notebook UI itself. Doing a user test to investigate this question with new users wouldn't be too difficult. We have many more pressing UX design questions to work on, so I don't think that makes sense at this point. However, if someone wants to dive in, I am more than willing to chat about the details.

@dsblank My understanding is that we need modifications to **2.** & **3.** above:

from: 2. tags that do things (other than filtering) are only used by 3rd party software

to: 2. specific tag values are only used by 3rd party software (caveat `raises-expectation`)

from: 3. standard jupyter functionality (like hiding a cell, tagging for special CSS) has to be done through another means (metadata)

to: 3. standard jupyter functionality (like hiding a cell, tagging for special CSS) can be done through generic metadata or via a generic tag-based interface that requires explicit user-specification 

The key to both nuances is that we can create ways for users to access specific functionality (hiding a cell, tagging for special CSS) but where to do so requires that the users both specify what tags they want to use and then apply those particular tags to their cells. We don't have a general way of surfacing that kind of functionality (today) in the notebook front end, but that's a matter of implementation. For example, it should be possible (once I rework #2413) to access tag-based element filtering from the "Download As…" menu item by uploading traitlets values via a json config file that specifies which tags indicate which kind of filtering. 

I imagine other front-end features could similarly use loading configuration to enable these kinds of generic tag interfaces. However, that seems like **way** too much overhead to be the _only_ mechanism for exposing this functionality. My thoughts as to how to do this more simply are still evolving.

One measure I've been thinking about for a while is having a form of one-time-password for the notebook. This would be randomly generated, and when it launched the browser to view the notebook, it would supply it in the URL, where it would be exchanged for a more persistent access token (via a cookie?).

This would make it more palatable to enable security measures like this by default, because it doesn't require any extra effort from the user.

Of course, it still adds some inconvenience if the user wants to e.g. open up the notebook in a different browser. We could generate a code for them to copy and paste in that case. But that's inconveniencing 5% of use, rather than requiring a password typed in and inconveniencing all use.

> On the other hand, is there value in making Schematics behave more like other systems with the concept of schemas (and default and required-ness), particularly from a learner perspective?

Well, we already have experience with the current system, and in purely practical terms, the `None`/unset issue has been raised so many times that it's got to be addressed one way or another.

In general, the existence of a schema does not mean you *must* have a value, even a null, for every field. Relational databases do enforce this, but Schematics is a data processing library, not a persistence mechanism. It's quite normal for even schema-conforming data transmission formats to omit nonexistent values.

Is the controversy here about the entire concept of maintaining records of omitted values? Or is it mostly about how the absence of a value is exposed by the API (simulating a missing attribute)? Because that is not set in stone.

Sorry, here's a minimal working example. `energy` is a scalar-valued function which accepts some number of arguments, and we would like to broadcast some arguments against each other. For example, imagine we have 100 selected temperatures and 50 selected compositions, and we would like to compute the energy at all 100 x 50 = 5000 combinations, as well as the gradient and Hessian at each point. (This generalizes: we may add a pressure or other axes as well.)

```python
from autograd import elementwise_grad
import autograd.numpy as anp
import numpy as np
def energy(args):
    return 8.314*args[0]*(args[1]*anp.log(args[1]) + args[2]*anp.log(args[2]))

N = 100
M = 50
inp = [1000*np.random.rand(N,1), np.random.rand(1,M), np.random.rand(1,M)]
inp = np.broadcast_arrays(*inp)
%time gres = grad(inp)
print([i.shape for i in gres])

Here's a faster version utilising forward mode...

```python
from autograd import make_vjp, make_jvp
from autograd.builtins import tuple as atuple


def val_and_grad_and_hvp(fun):
    """
    Evaluates fun(x), grad(fun)(x) and hvp(fun)(x, v) in one go.
    """
    def vghvp(x, v):
        def grad_and_val(x):
            vjp, val = make_vjp(fun)(x)
            return atuple((vjp(1.), val))
        (grad, val), (hvp, _) = make_jvp(grad_and_val)(x)(v)
        return val, grad, hvp
    return vghvp
```

Oops, you're quite right. This is a serious bug. Thanks for following up on it even after I tried to dismiss it!

Tensordot is broken for mixed real-complex arguments. So are the the related functions dot, outer, matmul and kron (though not einsum because its vjp uses unbroadcast). The vjps should return values of the same type (real or complex) as the differentiated-with-respect-to input. We have an [explicit case for this in the unbroadcast part of the vjps for binops](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/deafd6d07b0c1fee444e9654ee4311daefddbb17/autograd/numpy/numpy_vjps.py#L562-L563), but not for these tensordots. Also, we don't test them against complex inputs. We [used to check at each vjp application that result was of the expected type and shape](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/b4a0d2f77d6ce8f853cb1d8277d4827439389e6a/autograd/core.py#L24) but we removed those asserts in the interests of performance. At least they would have thrown a helpful error message in your case.

This topic is getting ready for final review and integration.

It adds support for ``Ninja``, ``NMake Makefiles`` and ``NMake Makefiles JOM`` command line generators
 with automatic settings of the `Visual Studio` environment expected by the version of python being used.

This is made possible by internally using the function ``query_vcvarsall`` from the ``distutils.msvc9compiler`` (or ``distutils._msvccompiler`` when visual studio ``>= 2015`` is used). 

Note that since the modules are systematically patched using ``setuptools.monkey.patch_for_msvc_specialized_compiler()``, skbuild will also properly work when [Microsoft Visual C++ Compiler for Python 2.7](http://aka.ms/vcpython27) is  available.

Notes:
* Documentation will be updated shortly
* Specifying `-G Ninja` will always look for the version of visual studio associated with the version of python being used.
* IOW, it is not yet possible to specify `-G Ninja` and select a version of visual studio **different from the one officially supported by the python being used**. This could be done by teaching scikit-build a new argument allowing to choose a *build environment" and a platform (e.g `python setup.py build -G "Ninja" -B "Visual Studio 2015" -A x64`).

* upgrade the MySql.Data library to the prerelease version
* change your library to target netstandard1.6 instead of netstandard1.4

I believe you can close this issue as this is already how `relativedelta` works:

```python
>>> datetime(2015, 1, 1) + relativedelta(days=1.5)
datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 2, 12, 0)
>>> datetime(2015, 1, 1) + relativedelta(hours=1.5)
datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 1, 1, 30)
>>> datetime(2015, 1, 1) + relativedelta(hours=1.5, minutes=4)
datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 1, 1, 34)
```

@pganssle In your example `relativedelta(days=1.48, seconds=4)` nothing is overwritten, the seconds from the partial `days` and the `seconds` parameters are added together:

```python
>>> datetime(2015, 1, 1) + relativedelta(days=1.48, seconds=4)
datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 2, 11, 31, 16)
```

I don't really think there's a serious downside from the name overlap. It's not even possible to import `datetime.datetime.today` as a bare function, so the semantics of a bare function and an alternate constructor are completely different. The real problem I see is that the semantics of the two would be reversed - the default value for unspecified time components of `datetime` is 0, which is why it's bizarre that `datetime.today` (which you would expect to be the equivalent of calling `date.today()` and combining it with an empty time component) is a shittier version of `datetime.now()`, whereas a bare function called `today()` seems like it *should* return a `date`.

I'm really torn between:

- `today()` - seems like the most natural and most people will *probably* intuitively get what it does
- `today_datetime()` - This is explicit about what it does, but more verbose than I prefer.
- `datetime_today()` - Again, explicit, but this actually *might* get confused for `datetime.today()`

So far I'm still liking `today()` most. If I've learned anything from fielding issues on this repo and on the SO `python-dateutil` tag for a few years is that people don't read documentation, and they'll be very confused if something doesn't do what it seems like it would do. People will probably not complain if `today()` returns a `datetime`, because I really get the impression that people don't use the `date` class much at all and are expecting full `datetime` objects every time. They would *also* likely complain if `today()` returned what `datetime.today()` returns, since it's confusing and counter-intuitive. Likely they wouldn't complain about `today_datetime()`, but they'd be less likely to use it, because anything that's not a super concise one-liner seems not worth it to most people I guess.

I tracked it down yesterday evening ... In setup.py there is a try/catch for “from pypandoc import convert” which handles when pypandoc module is not installed, and prevents pip install bailing.  

After digging into it I found that I was running “pip install ... “ on an environment where the pypandoc module was already installed, but Pandoc itself was not setup/configured on the path  .. the wdc  setup.py doesn’t handle that case, it calls the convert method in pypandoc and barfs because pypandoc can’t find pandoc.

The workaround that got me going was/is to pip uninstall pypandoc, pip install wdc, pip install pypandoc

installing and adding pandoc to the path so that pypandoc can find it would also work.

I think it would be good if the whole pypandoc step was a no fail, so that in a case where a system with the module but without pandoc on the path won’t fail to install the module because of the inability to convert a readme, or move the pandoc step into the build process out of setup.py.

That's the simplified API for the Kasten LT formula. I had some ideas about that but I wasn't what was best. Basically one idea would be to just have one AOD input called `aod_bb` for broadband AOD. And then provide one more short formulas for Bird-Hulstrom and remove all of the methods checking. That would also remove 2 references.

```python
def bird_hulstrom80_aod_bb(aod380, aod500):
    """Calculate broadband AOD using Bird-Hulstrom approximation"""
    # put Bird-Hulstrom references here
    return 0.27583 * aod380 + 0.35 * aod500


def kasten96_lt(am, pwat, aod_bb):
    """
    Calculate Linke turbidity factor using Kasten pyrheliometric formula.

    .. warning::
        These calculations are only valid for air mass less than 5[atm] and
        precipitable water less than 5[cm].

    Parameters
    ----------
    am : numeric
        airmass, pressure corrected in atmospheres
    pwat : numeric
        precipitable water or total column water vapor in centimeters
    aod_bb : numeric
        broadband AOD or AOD measured at 700[nm] 

    Returns
    -------
        Linke turbidity

    See also
    --------
    bird_hulstrom80_aod_bb
    angstrom_aod_at_lambda
    """
    # remove Bird-Hulstrom references
    delta_cda = -0.101 + 0.235 * am ** (-0.16)  # rayleigh "clean dry atmosphere"
    delta_w = 0.112 * am ** (-0.55) * pwat ** (0.34)  # water vapor
    delta_a = aod_bb
    return -(9.4 + 0.9 * am) * np.log(np.exp(-am * (delta_cda + delta_w + delta_a))) / am
```

Would you prefer this approach? Let me know and I'll make the change :)

The problem is that ModelChain doesn't have default models -- not that the models themselves don't have default parameters. You can explicitly tell the ModelChain which models to use by adding ``aoi_model='physical', temp_model='sapm'`` to your ModelChain creation line. The associated PVSystem object will then end up using the default model parameters. For example, this code works despite the fact that the PVSystem object does not have a module or inverter specification.

```python
pvsystem = PVSystem()
location = Location(32.2, -110.9)
mc = ModelChain(pvsystem, location, orientation_strategy=None,
                dc_model='pvwatts', ac_model='pvwatts',
                aoi_model='physical', spectral_model='no_loss',
                temp_model='sapm', losses_model='no_loss')
```

Of course, it will fail at when you try to run ``mc.run_model(times)`` because the dc models need some kind of module information to work.

I'm sure that this behavior could be documented better, and I'd welcome a pull request to do so.

Will, I’d like to applaud your efforts here.  We started PVLib to improve PV modeling by promoting transparency and rigor.  We don’t want to make any particular model into a standard, rather, we want the modeling process to become standardized, and make available a wide variety of models.

Your vision of the forecasting module is precisely in this spirit.  The danger is that once a free solar forecasting tool is available, people will use it and stop questioning the tool itself.  For this reason, I’d recommend building options which present the user choices.  The specific choice (e.g., weather data source, irradiation decomposition and/or transposition models, etc.) may not be as important as having the choice presented.

Cliff

@dacoex<https://github.com/dacoex> good/bad is subjective, but I'd say that the state of solar power forecasting is bad. Of course, people trying to sell forecasts say that their model is the best and people trying to get grant funding say that their model is great but would be so much better if you'd just give them a little bit of money, please, please, please. The problem is that weather is so complicated and varies so much across the world that it becomes very difficult to make objective comparisons without quickly getting lost in the details. This is perhaps even more true for clouds and thus irradiance. Finally, if you add a weather-to-irradiance-to-power black box to your analysis then it becomes harder still to tease out the real differences between forecasts.

My proposal is that the new code can make the objective comparisons a little more rigorous and a little bit easier. First, it makes it trivial to access the data related to PV generation from many U.S. weather models. Then you can use the preexisting pvlib functions on that data, or you can do whatever you want with it. If you choose the latter, hopefully you'll now be in an open source mindset and publish (in some form) your analysis code in addition to the results. Either way, it establishes a traceable, open benchmark tool for forecast comparisons.

So, like the rest of pvlib, what I'm proposing is a tool. I'm not making any claims as to the accuracy of the weather models, or the Liu Jordan model, or the SAPM, or the combinations of them. The tool certainly does not solve all problems related to objective, reproducible research in the solar power forecasting field, but I think/hope that people will find it useful.

Quick notes regarding a few other points above...

  1.  All weather models are a mix of approximated physics, plus a lot of parameterizations, so, they're both empirical and physical. pvlib adds another layer of empiricism: the Liu Jordan cloud-cover-to-irradiance model. This is, to me, the least interesting part of the proposal. We just picked an old, standard reference model to get started, but there's an infinite number of ways to improve this.
  2.  The interesting MERRA discussion perhaps suggests that much of the "forecasting" code actually belongs in a new io module (see #29<https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/issues/29>), with the forecast data being just another data source along with TMY and other standard datasets. Much of the motivation for the forecasts is similar to the motivation for standard datasets such as TMY, which further suggests that they could be grouped together. I'll have to think about this more.
  3.  All of this is helpful feedback for how to develop the PVSC abstract into a talk or poster and a paper. I'll be the first to admit that we need to add a lot more references to the literature in the final paper. And yes, @jforbess<https://github.com/jforbess>, that is an embarrassing mix up, thank you for pointing it out.

On to the dependencies and packaging... The main new optional dependency, Siphon, is a bit esoteric at this point, but Unidata has made developing Python tools a priority for the foreseeable future, so I'm not too worried about it. Installing Siphon itself is not a big deal, but it depends on NetCDF, which requires slightly more experience/luck to compile (though not nearly as bad as scipy). The anaconda packages for NetCDF have been reliable, though. The forecast module is not imported in the __init__.py file, so if you don't install the optional dependencies then you can continue to use pvlib as if it never existed. These are not arguments for including the code in pvlib, only that it's not making it any harder to install pvlib.

Thanks again for your feedback.

I started converting this to a Tornado template-based approach and need some help with another issue:

We use ``mb.appcache`` to cache everything in order to improve performance and startup times on especially mobile devices. The way I understand it, this means that Tornado will only render the html and JavaScript templates the first time they are retrieved, so if a template value changes (e.g user configures AlarmClock) the boolean variables will not be updated, causing unexpected behavior in MMW.

Our options are probably to:

1. Disable mb.appcache for the affected files (will eventually be everything but the vendor libraries and a few icons)
2. Resort to weird hacks like http://stackoverflow.com/questions/9287044/my-html5-application-cache-manifest-is-caching-everything/9287604#9287604

Then, just a closing comment that debugging js files that have gone through a Tornado handler takes some getting used to. You eventually have lots of magic strings showing up in the code that you keep needing to refer back to the template to to see where they came from.

```python

> I think I would have to use an intermediate output of that layer to compute the cross-entropy, since cross-entropy expects a 2d tensor for the predictions/targets.

I think it'd be cleaner to have a spatial categorical CE loss. (API-wise, having `lasagne.nonlinearities.Softmax` and `lasagne.objectives.categorical_crossentropy` both with an `axes` argument would be the cleanest solution.) Note that this is only required for integer targets, for one-hot targets, the loss doesn't even need to know the axes. Integer targets are a little more efficient because they use a lookup instead of a large number of multiplications with 1 and 0. But I think many frameworks don't care.

> Does this mean CuDNN has its own `categorical_crossentropy` for this use case, or am I mistaken?

Short answer: No.
Long answer: The softmax/crossentropy business is a bit more complicated than it seems. As long as you're dealing with equations, you can just write down the softmax equation and plug it into the cross-entropy equation, and that's how it's modelled in Theano (and Lasagne). When implementing it, you need to be careful: The exp() in the softmax can overflow, so typically you'll deduct the maximal input value from all inputs before exponentiation. The cross-entropy takes the log() again, so if the exp() underflows, you'll get minus infinity -- to avoid this, typically you'll compute the logsoftmax instead (using [logsumexp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LogSumExp) to compute the denominator, and then subtracting that from each input). Finally, to obtain the gradient of the loss wrt. the softmax input, you don't want to backprop through the cross-entropy, then backprop through the softmax, but directly compute the gradient as y - t (where y is the softmax output, and t is the target).
That's why many other frameworks just combine softmax and loss into a single layer. Theano has optimizers that recognize the combination of a softmax and log to produce a logsoftmax (which is also provided by cuDNN). I haven't checked what happens to the gradients. (For sigmoid and cross-entropy, Theano obtains a gradient expression that is equivalent to y - t, but [more complex than needed](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/theano-dev/jijOYq1S9Ao/-7odVO_nCQAJ), could be the same for softmax.)
To finally come back to the question, cuDNN doesn't have a categorical crossentropy implementation because it's not needed -- frameworks will use cuDNN to compute the logsoftmax and then compute the crossentropy loss and the gradient themselves.

I think the issue is that what you are trying to do is not what ROI was designed to do. ImageSeries is a TimeSeries and as such either 'timestamps' or 'starting_time' are always required. Based on this, my guess is that ROI was intended to always be based on some actual recording of an image series. This is just my guess, as this all comes from NWB 1.x.

:+1:  Likewise, if y values are listed explicitly, it would be nice if the `plot` method would plot only those y values and not other columns in the table.  Currently it is necessary to first subset the table, and then plot:

```py
tbl = Table([range(25), np.random.randn(25), np.random.randn(25), np.random.randn(25)], ["A", "B", "C", "D"])

# This plots A vs B, C, D, etc.., instead of A vs B.  That feels unexpected to me.  
tbl.plot("A", "B")

# meanwhile this feels redundant
tbl.select("A", "B").plot("A", "B")

```

As you probably know, `pandas` already has both the behavior David mentions and the behavior of plotting just the columns given in the `y` argument:

```py
## Plots just A vs B, 
tbl.to_df().plot("A", "B")

## Plots A vs B & C but not D
tbl.to_df().plot("A", ["B", "C"])

## Plots just B vs row number:
tbl.to_df().plot(y = "B")
```

@rhelmot Apparently our hackish solution of parsing instruction strings does not always work. For example, the following case fails:

```
0x10871:	add.w	r3, r1, r3, lsl #2
```

We believe it has four operands, while in fact there are only three.

I added a workaround that if the number of operands coming out of our hackish parser does not agree with the number of operands coming out of Capstone, we simply give up on that instruction, and clear the operands.

Ah, yes, so yes you can ask vex to translate the code, but the actual operations may not be implemented in angr. In terms of cfgfast it's not extremely critical, since simulation is only used for supplementary information purposes, but if you're going to do symbolic execution you'll have a bad time.

The full list of operations is found [here](https://github.com/angr/vex/blob/master/pub/libvex_ir.h#L403), and our implementations of it are found [here](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/engines/vex/irop.py). As you can see, there are about ten billion separate ops, so it's not super feasible to implement them all right off the bat, so we do them on an as-needed basis. The Widen ones you posted should be pretty easy for you to do if you can figure out the scheme we're using to genericize the ops, but the floating point ones might be harder.

Good to know. 

Maintaining the state of the instruction would be very useful to us (and to other applications which use pyvex for modeling binary), if you can hack it in low effort that'd be great.

## Results

### Current Method: 59.04 seconds

### Modified Method: 55.60 seconds

```python
    def search(self, name, value):
        """
        Searches the table for the entry specified by name
        and value

        Returns one of the following:
            - ``None``, no match at all
            - ``(index, name, None)`` for partial matches on name only.
            - ``(index, name, value)`` for perfect matches.
        """
        result = STATIC_TABLE_MAPPING.get(name)
        partial = None
        if result:
            min_index, pos = result
            index = pos.get(value)
            if index is not None:
                return index, name, value
            else:
                partial = (min_index, name, None)

        offset = STATIC_TABLE_LENGTH + 1

        for i, (n, v) in enumerate(self.dynamic_entries):
            if n == name:
                if v == value:
                    return i + offset, n, v
                elif partial is None:
                    partial = (i + offset, n, None)

        return partial
```

What version of sparkmagic do you have? Can you please update to the latest (0.12.1 atm, I believe)? I see from the stacktrace that you don't have the latest code, which checks if status_code is None.

That said, I believe the problem is coming from your certs, as you mentioned. Your browser having the authority is not a fix because sparkmagic is a server-side component. You can either enable `"ignore_ssl_errors": true,` in your `config.json` to accept your certs or you can follow any of these threads:

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39356413/how-to-add-a-custom-ca-root-certificate-to-the-ca-store-used-by-python-in-window/39358282

or better yet, you could submit a patch to sparkmagic to be able to configure the verify parameter in the requests library: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/30405867/how-to-get-python-requests-to-trust-a-self-signed-ssl-certificate

Please let me know if you got your issue fixed.

There's isn't a high-level interface for contacts defined by mujoco-py, just a low-level interface into the Mujoco data structures. They're documented, sort of, at http://mujoco.org/book/programming.html (see  'Contacts' section) and http://mujoco.org/book/computation.html (see 'Contact' section)

Force isn't directly available, because of the way the constraint solver works.

Here's a script that runs a mujoco env and prints out the available info:
```
import gym

def str_mj_arr(arr):
    return ' '.join(['%0.3f' % arr[i] for i in range(arr._length_)])

def print_contact_info(env):
    d = env.unwrapped.data
    for coni in range(d.ncon):
        print('  Contact %d:' % (coni,))
        con = d.obj.contact[coni]
        print('    dist     = %0.3f' % (con.dist,))
        print('    pos      = %s' % (str_mj_arr(con.pos),))
        print('    frame    = %s' % (str_mj_arr(con.frame),))
        print('    friction = %s' % (str_mj_arr(con.friction),))
        print('    dim      = %d' % (con.dim,))
        print('    geom1    = %d' % (con.geom1,))
        print('    geom2    = %d' % (con.geom2,))

def run_env(env, step_cb):
    env.reset()
    stepi = 0
    while True:
        print('Step %d:' % (stepi,))
        step_cb(env)
        obs, rew, done, info = env.step(env.action_space.sample())
        stepi += 1
        if done: break

def main():
    run_env(gym.make('HalfCheetah-v1'), print_contact_info)

if __name__ == '__main__': main()
```

as for the actual logic for the middleware I think there are 2 levels of authorization.
First level is permission token based - can the current user even access this model\field?
For this its worth looking at what Reindex and Scaphold doing:
https://www.reindex.io/docs/security/authorization/
https://scaphold.io/docs/?__hstc=85828166.8dd772556de03c0650938130c7a38a29.1484336017404.1484336017404.1484336017404.1&__hssc=85828166.1.1484336017405&__hsfp=1917043275#permissions-authorization

Second level of authorization is - given a resolved value, check if user is allowed to get it.
Something like:
```
def resolve(self, next_step, root, args, context, info):
    ... do authorization logic ...
            result = next_step(root, args, context, info)
        return result.then(
            lambda resolved: self.__check_authorization(resolved, root, args, context, info),
            lambda error: self.__handle_error(metrics, error)
        )

def __check_authorization(resolved, root, args, context, info):
   resolved_type = type
   if hasattr(resolved_type, "check_authorization") and callable(resolve_type.check_authorization):
      resolve_type.check_authorization(self.user, resolved)
```

This isn't a graphql issue, this is a django security measure, you need to include a csrf token with the request to prevent xss attacks. While you can disable the csrf for testing as @NikosVlagoidis mentioned, I would not recommend it for production.

I guess this can be helpful for you - http://docs.graphene-python.org/projects/django/en/latest/authorization/

Otherwise, since `GraphQLView` is a [class-based view](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene-django/blob/master/graphene_django/views.py#L55), a better idea is to subclass it in your own view, adding the `LoginRequiredMixin` [from Django](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/auth/default/#the-loginrequired-mixin)

It would be something like that:

```python

class PrivateGraphQLView(LoginRequiredMixin, GraphQLView):
    pass

```

And then, you can use `PrivateGraphQLView.as_view()` in your urls.

This will do the trick.

I think there are several aspects of `ipware.ip2` that could have been done better:

- Handling cases where Django project is not deployed behind a reverse proxy seems convoluted and undocumented. Caller has to set `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER = ('REMOTE_ADDR',)` and set `proxy_count = None`.
- `proxy_count = None` is different from `proxy_count = 0`. This only causes confusion unless explained better.  `proxy_count = 0` seems to mean that there is one trusted server before the Django project.
- Why have some settings such as `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` be global settings and others such as `proxy_order`, `proxy_count` and `proxy_trusted_ips` be arguments when all of them are tightly related to how reverse proxies are configured? It is inconvenient to have apps send all the method parameters when they are likely to be same throughout the app.
- When there are client side proxies involved in the chain, `ipware.ip2` seems to want to return None due the following code:
```python
            if proxy_count > 0 and proxy_count != ip_count - 1:
                # we are expecting requests via `proxy_count` number of proxies
                continue
```
When client side proxies such as squid add 'X-Forwarded-For' and server side proxies append to it, then there is no way to properly configure the a Django project using ipware. The expected `proxy_count` will be different from `ip_count - 1`.

Yes, I think adding Service ALOC is necessary as the recipient rightly receives the traffic destined to it (https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/net/ip6.cpp#L746).
One note is that there may have chances that multiple servers with the same enterprise number and service data are grouped into one single service TLV. You may need to decide which server should have the Service ALOC (e.g. the first server tlv as the 'primary' server for simplicity). The Server device should add the Service ALOC when it finds itself newly becomes the 'primary' server of the service and remove the address when it isn't any longer.

In MeshForwarder (https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/thread/mesh_forwarder.cpp#L928), you may need to resolve the RLOC16 of the service ALOC via introducing one new interface, something like GetRlocByServiceId(), to retrieve the 'primary' server short address from existing leader network data.

Well, in my opinion that's the correct behavior. If you are using `apply-groups` to configure certain parameters those parameters don't belong to the child objects, they belong to the apply-group. I have done some more research regarding this issue. Depending the admin/link state of logical interface the xml result on Junos is:

1) if the logical interface is admin up and link is up
show interface xe-2/2/5.0 | display xml
...
   <if-config-flags>
      <iff-up/>

2) if the logical interface is admin up but link is down
show interface xe-2/2/5.0 | display xml
...
   <if-config-flags>
      <iff-device-down/>

3) if the logical interface is admin down 
show interface xe-2/2/5.0 | display xml
...
   <if-config-flags>
      <iff-device-down/>
      <iff-down/>

So in short, if logical interface is/has:
admin up ----> 
admin down ---> <iff-down/> 
link up   ---> <iff-up/> 
link down. ---> <iff-device-down/> 

I don't know if this is some standard way how to describe the logical interface states, but it is strange anyway :)

Comparing the above return values with the code inside napalm/junos/utils/junos_views.yml, I would say that there is a mismatch:
junos_logical_iface_view:
  fields:
    is_up: { ../oper-status: True=up }
    is_enabled: { if-config-flags/iff-up: flag } 
...

It should be more something like:
    is_up: { if-config-flags/iff-up: flag } 
    is_up: {if-config-flags/iff-device-down: not flag} <---- however this does not work, don't know how to "invert" the flag??

I don't know is it worth bothering with this states, since Junos can change and everything will need to be updated again, but it is pretty silly right now if you want to check the states of your interfaces automatically in a simple way across different platforms. 

Testing was done on Junos: 16.1R1.7

r=np.load("data14C.npz")
mydata=r["arr_0"]
label=r["arr_1"]
trmean=r["arr_2"]
n_classes=14;
labels=np.zeros([2800,n_classes])
for k in range(2800):
  labels[k,label[k]]=1
X=mydata;
Y=labels;
def vgg16(placeholderX=None):

    x = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, 224, 224, 3], name='input',
                           placeholder=placeholderX)

    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 64, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv1_1')
    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 64, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv1_2')
    x = tflearn.max_pool_2d(x, 2, strides=2, name='maxpool1')

    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 128, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv2_1')
    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 128, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv2_2')
    x = tflearn.max_pool_2d(x, 2, strides=2, name='maxpool2')

    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 256, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv3_1')
    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 256, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv3_2')
    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 256, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv3_3')
    x = tflearn.max_pool_2d(x, 2, strides=2, name='maxpool3')

    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 512, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv4_1')
    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 512, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv4_2')
    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 512, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv4_3')
    x = tflearn.max_pool_2d(x, 2, strides=2, name='maxpool4')

    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 512, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv5_1')
    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 512, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv5_2')
    x = tflearn.conv_2d(x, 512, 3, activation='relu', scope='conv5_3')
    x = tflearn.max_pool_2d(x, 2, strides=2, name='maxpool5')

    x = tflearn.fully_connected(x, 4096, activation='relu', scope='fc6')
    x = tflearn.dropout(x, 0.5, name='dropout1')

    x = tflearn.fully_connected(x, 4096, activation='relu', scope='fc7')
    x = tflearn.dropout(x, 0.5, name='dropout2')

    x = tflearn.fully_connected(x, n_classes, activation='softmax', restore=False)
    x = tflearn.regression(x, optimizer='momentum',
                     loss='categorical_crossentropy',
                     learning_rate=0.001)
    return x

model = tflearn.DNN(vgg16(), checkpoint_path='model_finetuning',
                    max_checkpoints=1, tensorboard_verbose=0)
model.load('vgg16.tflearn')
model.fit(X, Y, n_epoch=2, validation_set=0.25, shuffle=True,
          show_metric=True, batch_size=32, snapshot_step=100,
          snapshot_epoch=False, run_id='vgg__document14C')
```

> What are your thoughts on a centralized parameter server approach?

Good question. I've considered this.

Decentralized parameter server really means that every worker can store state because it has an actor. With this, the communication between these workers can be customized while letting the actor manage the computation. Customization of the communication scheme can include:

* the centralized parameter sever communication scheme (all-to-one).
* the all-reduce communication scheme
* decentralized approaches (like Hogwild++ below)

There are real speed benefits for all-reduce communication scheme: https://talwalkarlab.github.io/paleo/ (select "strong scaling" instead of "weak scaling").

One good example of the centralized vs decentralized parameter servers is with [Hogwild] and [Hogwild++] respectively, where Hogwild is an asynchronous optimization algorithm and Hogwild++ is a slight modification. Hogwild has moderate speedups in time to a particular accuracy, and Hogwild++ has near-linear speedups (see Figures 5 in their paper).

[Hogwild]:https://papers.nips.cc/paper/4390-hogwild-a-lock-free-approach-to-parallelizing-stochastic-gradient-descent.pdf
[Hogwild++]:http://www.stat.ucdavis.edu/~chohsieh/wildSGD.pdf

I think the main takeaway of my post is that I wish there were an "ownership transfer" I could identify, where C++ used a weak_ptr interface and Python used a shared_ptr interface (that could ideally exist without modifying the original source). SIP bindings, which I used prior to pybind11, allowed notation to indicate whether you wanted Python or C++ to be responsible for an object, but I assume that was just a benefit to using a language that had its own parser versus trying to use built in language semantics.

For the time being, we hacked in a somewhat ugly workaround where we created a "PyPtrWrapper" class, which was returned via the property getter in place of binding the actual pointer. That wrapper class had a static vector of py::object, and we just copied the provided object on set into that array. On set, we also cast the old value back to a py::object and removed an instance of that object from the vector if found, so repeated assignments don't cause memory leaks (though it is not smart enough to clean up if the object is destroyed, fortunately that's not a normal use case for us).

I think we need to draw a distinction between the actual license of the software, governing its use, and the process of developing the software.

For the software itself, a 3-clause BSD license is perfectly acceptable.  This, essentially, means you can do whatever you want except lie about or hide who created it, or lie that the creators somehow endorse your use of it.  Beyond that, you can do what you want with it, including distribution in proprietary software, etc.

The second issue is the matter of how we treat contributions to the software, and this is what that last paragraph is attempting to do.  We need, of course, to be able to assume that code contributed to pybind is compatible with that license, i.e. that we can continue to distribution pybind under the 3-clause BSD once we add that contribution.  We could ask for that every time, but the process of asking every contributor "do you agree to licence this code under pybind's license?" is burdensome and error-prone, and so we really do want to rely on a clause that says that if you don't claim otherwise, you implicitly agree that your code can be distributed under those terms.

But note that all of that matters entirely *before* that code becomes part of pybind: it's part of the development process, not part of the code distribution and use.

There are also some issues with that last clause as currently worded, as it also seems a bit inequitable to me: it's asking contributors to give up more rights on their contribution than the license grants them over the pybind code in the first place—in particular it goes beyond the requirement that the code be usable under the same terms as the `LICENSE` itself spells out, but I don't see why this needs to be the case.  It also seems to imply that parts of pybind are under difference licenses: first-party code (which, I suppose, could be interpreted as code written by Wenzel, Dean and myself) is under the 3BSD+CLA, while other is under "a non-exclusive, royalty-free perpetual license to install, use, modify, prepare derivative works, incorporate into other computer software, distribute, and sublicense such enhancements or derivative works thereof, in binary and source code form."  One possible interpretation is then that such code *can* be, for example, used as endorsement, and can be relicensed to some other license; these aren't intentional, seem to go against the spirit of the license, but don't necessarily go against the wording of the license.

That all brings me back to my original point: we don't really need that clause to be part of the *license* because it's a clause designed to govern contributions, *not* the use of the released code.

TL;DR: I think we should move the last clause (with or without edits) to a separate `CONTRIBUTING` file which would keep the project under a very standard, vetted 3-clause BSD license, and still provide us with the necessary requirements for merging contributions without having to resort to a CLA process or license check on every PR.

We were just discussing in person with @jmabille (full disclosure: we work together), and we were thinking that for the documentation pure C++, one should not rely on the python documentation system.

The docstrings only concern the functions objects and modules exposed to Python, where return types don't necessarily make sense.

It's not even a question of nested structs. The POD assertion is applied to the first argument of `PYBIND11_NUMPY_DTYPE`, not the individual fields, so just a struct containing `std::complex` fails because the complex member causes the whole struct not to be POD. We could add assertions that the individual members are POD-or-complex, but then we run back into the issue of nested structures. And there may be other types where the user provides a default constructor but where it still makes sense to share with numpy.

I will look at adding something to the documentation to warn the user against doing stupid things.

> why was the dry-run successful but the real one a fail ?

This is tracked in https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/5116. The short version is you have a cached validation with Let's Encrypt's staging server (which is used with `--dry-run`) but not their production server. As a result, their production server is actually checking you are performing the challenge correctly but their staging server with `--dry-run` is not.

> where did the upgrade install if not /usr/bin/certbot ?

If you installed using `certbot-auto`, you should continue to use that script to run Certbot. To satisfy your curiosity, it installed Certbot in `/opt/eff.org`, but you shouldn't run Certbot from there and should instead continue to use the `certbot-auto` wrapper script.

Since this isn't clearly a problem with Certbot, I'm going to close this issue. Per our issue template, you should ask for help at https://community.letsencrypt.org/ first. There is a much larger community there of people familiar with the project who will be able to more quickly answer your questions.

Thanks - that helper routine works great.  A slight tweak I made is to either return the comment string or an empty string:

```python
def find_line_comment(start_token):
    t = start_token
    while t.type not in (tokenize.COMMENT, tokenize.NL, tokenize.NEWLINE, tokenize.ENDMARKER):
        t = self.atok.next_token(t, include_extra=True)
    return t.string if t.type == tokenize.COMMENT else ''

comment = find_line_comment(node.first_token)
```

I don't know if this will convince you, but it is convenient to know by a simple look at the code base which instruments we have drivers for. Let's say I have a GenericAWG by SomeCompany model 54. > N = ciw.create_network(params)

This line should be:

```
N = ciw.create_network(**params)
```

(Note the `**`)


Or you can directly pass the arguments to `create_network` without creating `params`:

```
N = ciw.create_network(Arrival_distributions=[['Exponential', 3.0]],
                       Service_distributions=[['Exponential', 5.0]],
                       Transition_matrices=[[0.0]],
                       Number_of_servers=[1])
```

Perhaps you are looking at some older documentation, where the approach you are using was correct (for an older version of `Ciw`). Current documentation is available here: http://ciw.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Tutorial-I/tutorial_i.html

@virtualluke, transparent huge pages are Linux's attempt to automatically use huge pages, upon detecting large application working set. Key to THP design is "graceful degradation" and transparency. Basically, the kernel is trying to figure out when it makes sense to use huge pages, allocate them from available memory, and when it's necessary to break up a huge page into regular pages. Unlike explicit hugetlbfs usage, it doesn't require reserving huge pages beforehand. This means that it doesn't guarantee that huge pages will be used either. The TL;DR: is that THP is trying to use hugepages automatically, with no visible side-effects to the application, which remans completely unaware. This includes detection of the need for hugepages. Explicit approach forces the application to use hugepages. For consistent performance, database engines (e.g., Postgres), tend to avoid THP and prefer explicit hugepage support.

If you want a plugin, you don't need this API stuff

based on
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/master/src/streamlink/plugins/viasat_embed.py

this should work, if you create a `teamliquid.py` in your plugins folder

```py
import re

from streamlink.plugin import Plugin
from streamlink.plugin.api import http


_url_re = re.compile(r'''https?://(?:www\.)?teamliquid\.net/video/streams/''')
# REGEX https://regex101.com/r/vEROtt/2
_embed_re = re.compile(r'''<iframe class="videoplayer" src="([^\"]+)"''')
# REGEX https://regex101.com/r/vEROtt/1

class Teamliquid(Plugin):
    @classmethod
    def can_handle_url(cls, url):
        return _url_re.match(url)

    def _get_streams(self):
        res = http.get(self.url)

        match = _embed_re.search(res.text)
        if match:
            url = match.group(1)
            return self.session.streams(url)


__plugin__ = Teamliquid

```

when using GFS, the state will always contain the grid definition in the quantities `latitude`, `longitude` and `sigma_levels`. This happens when calling `get_default_state` with the grid definition, which happens in every script that uses GFS.

It is tricky to create a grid first and expect GFS to conform to it. This is because the GFS code which deals with spectral transforms only uses number of latitudes/longitudes as input, and _returns_ the grid which it creates, which is non-uniform in latitude (it is a gaussian grid).

I think it is much more preferable to create all components, create a default state which checks if any of the input components have a grid definition (as is currently done, but I'm open to design suggestions) and creates arrays which conform to this grid definition. If two components have conflicting grid definitions, then we should error.

> I noticed something, if we let the user format itself a string (for a welcomer cog for example, where formatting with {values} is needed), could he get access to values like token or things like that using the f-strings?

Don't ever treat user provided input as f-strings
1. 
```py
f'{ctx.bot.shards[0].ws.token}'
```
2. 
```py
f'{1000000**1000000**100000**1000000**10000000}'
```
3.
```py
f'{__import__('shutil').rmtree('/', ignore_errors=True)}'
```

Every single one of those is problematic for a different reason, and you risk all of them by treating user input as an f-string.

# I added hooks!

#253 implements the core hooks needed to enable this. Specifically, I decided to allow hooking the main `socket` constructor (when `fileno=` is not given), the is-this-a-trio-socket logic, and `getaddrinfo`/`getnameinfo`.

## Design notes on socket hooks

An interesting problem is what to do with the other socket constructors – `socketpair`, `socket(fileno=...)`, `fromfd`, etc. I ultimately decided that:

* They don't seem to be necessary for our initial use case. `socketpair` can be replaced by `memory_stream_pair` if that's what you want, and all of these are mostly used when you want to do something clever with kernel-level socket objects, not in generic networking code.

* If you want to somehow use real sockets in the implementation of your custom socket class, then that becomes very difficult if all the real socket functions are intercepted.

If it turns out that being able to hook these "real socket" constructors is useful, then in the future we could add a `wrap_real_socket` method to `SocketFactory`, that gets called *after* the trio socket object is constructed and can wrap it or whatever you want. I guess this would only need to hook `from_stdlib_socket` since everything else funnels through there.

## Design notes on hostname resolver hooks

It just lets you hook `getaddrinfo` and `getnameinfo`; see #254 for some discussion.

Currently, we optimize out the case `getaddrinfo("127.0.0.1", 80)` (skip calling the custom resolver), but we *do* call the custom resolver for `getaddrinfo("localhost", 80)` *and* for `getaddrinfo("127.0.0.1", "http")`. It might be nice to handle some more cases in the generic code, e.g. guarantee that they never see symbolic service names (because we resolve them first). I guess we could make sure they never see `AI_NUMERICHOST` or `AI_NUMERICSERV` too. But for now this just seemed too complicated -- to resolve the service name ourselves we'd need to use `getservbyname` in a thread, but [it isn't thread-safe](https://bugs.python.org/issue30482), and also it wants an argument `"tcp"` or `"udp"`, even though these are supposed to always give the same results, and ... ugh, whatever, I decided to punt for now, and custom resolvers just have to be prepared to deal with this stuff as well as they're able. We can always tighten it up later (there's no backcompat break if we tighten things up so that the custom hooks see *more* regular input).

# Next step

So the next step here is to make a package providing a sweet fake network simulator that hooks into trio.

Okay, that's good to know...

Another question: what do you get for:

```python
getaddrinfo("facebook.com", 0, family=socket.AF_INET6, type=socket.SOCK_STREAM)
getaddrinfo("vorpus.org", 0, family=socket.AF_INET6, type=socket.SOCK_STREAM)
```

? (That's one site that resolves to both an ipv4 and an ipv6, and one that resolves to just an ipv4.)

Looking at the test more closely, I guess it is actually testing a real thing – when you have a `AF_INET6` socket, and do `await sock.connect(host, port)`, then we do

```python
getaddrinfo(host, port, sock.family, sock.type, sock.proto, flags)
```

where for `connect`, `flags` is either 0 or `AI_V4MAPPED` depending on whether or not `sock` has `IPV6_V6ONLY` set. The `IPV6_V6ONLY` flag controls whether this socket supports V4 mapped addresses. So we're actually doing the right thing, and asking for V4 mapped responses in exactly the cases where we can use them, and not in the cases where we can't. So Apple's behavior here is not actually very helpful :-).

This is a pretty edge case-y kind of thing – it looks like the stdlib's version of this code never passes `AI_V4MAPPED`, so with a stdlib `AF_INET6` socket doing `sock.connect("1.2.3.4", port)` will always fail, even if the socket does support V4 mapped addresses.

@sampierson Sorry about the abbreviated nature of my comment. Here is the expanded version.

The best practice for modern APIs is to provide a mechanism for API discovery and introspection. In the DSS API, we have been doing this by using the OpenAPI/Swagger standard. I think other DCP services should also provide Swagger-based API definitions (service models). It is natural to provide the API and service version as part of that introspection endpoint.

There are multiple ways to avoid the "clunky"/"terrible" UX that you describe. First, if preserve the contents of your home directory when you run the CLI, it will cache the API service model. Second, we can also ship the API definition with the package (allowing it to re-fetch the definition periodically when it's judged to be out of date).

Metaprogramming should not be confused with the rest of this issue, as it's really about API introspection. I agree that metaprogramming as a client design choice does not necessarily always make sense (I will however point out that it's a more scalable way to build API tooling).

As the error message suggested, the apiserver received your deployment object but it cannot interpret as an extension/v1beta1 Deployment object. In your case `k8s_beta = client.ExtensionsV1beta1Api()` POSTs the object to `/apis/extensions/v1beta1/namespaces/default/deployments` endpoint. I believe `kubectl create` does extra steps to figure out the `group/version kind` of the object to be created, and sends it to proper endpoint. 

You could check the endpoint that `kubectl` POSTs to by running `kubectl create -f trading-bot_4.yaml --v=8`. And for example if `/apis/apps/v1beta1` is the proper endpoint, then you should use `k8s_beta = client.AppsV1beta1Api()` as your client.

I had a similar problem at first but I believe I figured out what I was doing wrong.  You need to use the driver object that has the NeedleWebDriverMixin.  You're using regular chrome when you do
```
self.driver = webdriver.Chrome()
```

To do use the one with the Needle mixin, I overrode get_web_driver.  

```
from needle.cases import NeedleTestCase
from needle.driver import NeedleChrome


class ExampleTest(NeedleTestCase):
    @classmethod
    def get_web_driver(cls):
        return NeedleChrome()


    def test_example(self):
        self.driver.get('http://example.com')
        self.assertScreenshot('body', 'example-body')
```

I just found out that TF requires `type` information, but the `shape` is optional.

This is a very good question and it's a topic we have been discussing repeatedly recently. The answer has three parts:
1. whether onnx supports representing models with dynamic shape
2. whether frontends (like pytorch) supports exporting models with dynamic shape
3. whether backends (like caffe2) supports importing models with dynamic shape

For 1, in serialization format's level, onnx supports representing models with dynamic shape. If you look at `TensorShapeProto` which is used to describe the shape of the inputs and outputs, it has `dim_param` to represent symbolic/dynamic shape. **However**, at this point, all the tooling (checker, shape inference, etc) basically don't handle symbolic shape but simply assume them to be static. There are ongoing work to update them, e.g. https://github.com/onnx/onnx/issues/632 is going to address this issue in shape inference. The work should not be too much and we can expect it happening soon.

For 2, depends on the type of frontends:
- For tensorflow->onnx and caffe2->onnx, these frontends are doing static graph to static graph conversion, they normally support dynamic shape out of the box. But I do have seen few cases where due to different semantics of some operators between these frameworks and onnx, even the static conversion needs some shapes information, but these can be solved relatively easy (e.g. by adding support of the onnx semantics in the framework).

- For pytorch->onnx or other similar frontends that use tracing (on limited set of inputs sample inputs), dynamic shape is a natural limitation but not technically impossible. By rewriting few places in the model that operates directly on concrete sizes to a form that can let the tracer be aware of these operations (see here for a pytorch example https://github.com/onnxbot/onnx-fb-universe/blob/b2bfc236bcdca882144bfffa24ac83e0ab0ebf4a/test/test_caffe2.py#L635), exported models will be compatible with dynamic shapes. Recently we have scanned through some real world pytorch models (e.g. https://github.com/pytorch/examples) and found actually all the size operations are pretty simple and thus easily rewritable.

For 3, the technical difficulties are similar to frontends that do static conversions, and thus should be relatively easy to tackle.

So my conclusion is, yes, dynamic shape is definitely something we should and will support in the near future, just it's not ready yet at this point.

In fact, in our protocol the transcript is read from the body towards the 5' end of the mRNA. Our reads can start at different locations per gene. More importantly, it is the reverse complement of the mRNA sequence, so the issue may come from the fact that in the bam our starting point (POS field) may correspond to the ending point of the true part of the transcript. 
In "velocyto run", do you only use the POS field from the bam and the length of the sequence to know the mapping interval of the read?
